channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: What’s a vacation for, anyway?
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
Most schools around the country are a couple of weeks into the new term and already it must feel overwhelming! I know I feel that way when the academic year begins after the summer vacation; a month or five weeks is barely enough to catch your breath. Those who are not in the education system think that getting a regular vacation is such a great privilege that any complaints about workload are nothing more than an indulgence. I won’t deny that there used to be some truth to that. In the past, teachers may have had the luxury of switching off during vacation. They could actually turn to other, equally important obligations such as family, or even indulge in some real holiday activity for more than a rushed weekend. But these days, largely due to the Internet and our smart phones, it’s hard to completely switch off from the looming presence of work. Add to that the expectations that we “productively use” the holidays to catch up on reading, prepare materials, and update any number of records that schools are required to maintain for documentation.

But hard does not equal impossible. We must find a way to guard against the takeover of our life by work, to protect the little downtime we have – and that we are owed. Those breaks between school years, and that shorter but still much-needed pause between terms? By all means, use them to catch up on subject-related reading and to hone your teaching ideas, but prioritize the down time to recharge your batteries – emotional, psychological, physical. Let’s not forget that being an educator, while rewarding, can be extremely sapping on all these dimensions.

Speaking of the school calendar, we recently heard from Sathyanarayanan Mundayoor, an old friend of the magazine who runs the Lohit Youth Library Network in Arunachal Pradesh. Responding to our geography special, he writes:

“Since Teacher Plus May 2025 carried a Geography issue, an interesting aspect struck me: Has our school education system been considering geographical factors of our people & their lives, when the school calendar was/is evolved? Aren't we still following in many states, what the ruling power had found convenient to them? I see an exception in Assam – July being a rice plantation month in Assam, it is a month of Annual vacation, apart from 2 weeks during Bihu (Apr 15) and 2 weeks in Dassara (Navaratri). Also NE Himalayan states like Meghalaya and Nagaland schools have holidays in Dec & January – the winter & Christmas months.”

He believes it may be productive to re-think the suitability of our school calendar (particularly as imposed nationwide by central boards like the CBSE) for the varied geographic and cultural contexts of our country. We’d love to hear what you think about this, and what impact it has on how children, particularly those in rural areas, deal with school and examinations. How does extreme weather or the agricultural cycle affect school attendance, for instance?

We take the calendar for granted, as something that cannot be changed, something that just is. But is it?

Khair, as the Hindi-speakers say. Or whatever, as our children say. We plug on, but it is good to question the assumptions upon which our routines are based, don’t you think?
item.excerpt:encoded: Most schools around the country are a couple of weeks into the new term and already it must feel overwhelming! I know I feel that way when the academic year begins after the summer vacation; a month or five weeks is barely enough to catch your breath.

item.title: Helping young readers
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Govinda Menaka

In today’s fast-paced, tech-filled world, it’s no surprise that reading doesn’t always come easy for kids. Between smart phones, social media, and the pressure to fit in, young readers are navigating a lot. If we want to help them fall in love with books—and stick with them—we need to understand what they’re looking for and what might be standing in their way.

What do kids want in a book?

Today’s readers aren’t just looking for any story—they want books that feel real to them, stories that reflect their lives, personalities, and even their dreams. Here’s what tends to draw them in:

Representation that feels true

Kids want to see characters that look like them, live like them, or come from families like theirs. When books reflect a child’s identity, they feel seen and valued (Bishop, 1990).

A sense of adventure

From fantasylands to real-world adventures, stories that whisk kids away from their everyday lives give them a place to dream big (Nikolajeva, 2014).

Humour and playfulness

Funny books are a hit—especially ones with silly characters, clever dialogue, or awkward moments that make kids laugh out loud (Lukens et al., 2013).

Relatable struggles

Whether it’s dealing with a best friend fallout or a tough day at school, stories that show kids facing real problems can offer both comfort and insight (Trelease, 2013).

Visuals that pop

Graphic novels, illustrated chapter books, and picture books still rule the shelves. The mix of visuals and storytelling keeps kids engaged—especially those who are visual learners (Short, 2011).

Books that inspire

Kids are drawn to stories that show courage, kindness, and resilience. These books help them believe they can be strong, too (Applegate & Applegate, 2010).

The challenges kids face with reading

Even kids who want to read might struggle to stick with books that are just right for their age. Some common hurdles include:

Trying to fit in

Peer pressure plays a big role. Some kids pick up books that seem “cool” or more mature, even if they aren’t quite ready for them yet (Guthrie & Wigfield, 2000).

Reading level gaps

Not every child reads at the same level, and that’s okay. But it can feel discouraging if a book is too tough—or too easy (Allington, 2012).

Screens everywhere

With video games, YouTube, and social media always a tap away, slower-paced reading can feel less exciting in comparison (Rideout, 2017).

Trying to seem older

Kids often want to “read up” to seem more grown-up. But jumping into books with mature themes too soon can leave them confused or overwhelmed (Galda et al., 2010).

Missing the connection

Sometimes the book just doesn’t click. When kids can’t relate to the characters or storyline, their interest fades (Ivey & Johnston, 2013).

How we can support young readers

So, how do we help? The good news: there’s a lot we can do to help kids find joy in reading—and stick with it.

Offer a variety of genres

Let them try mysteries, sci-fi, graphic novels, poetry—whatever catches their eye. Exploring different types of books keeps things exciting (Tunnell & Jacobs, 2017).

Help without hovering

Support struggling readers with tools like audio books or read-along apps, but let them set the pace. Confidence builds when reading feels doable (Allington & Gabriel, 2012).

Take the pressure off

Let them know it’s okay to read books that match where they are, not where they think they should be. Progress looks different for every kid (Trelease, 2013).

Make it a judgment-free zone

Create a space—at home or in the classroom—where all reading is celebrated. Whether it’s a chapter book or a comic, if they’re reading, they’re winning (Miller, 2009).

Use tech to your advantage

Instead of fighting screens, use them. E-books, apps like Epic!, or audio books with sound effects can make stories come alive (Rideout, 2017).

Conclusion

Helping kids become readers isn’t just about handing them the “right” books—it’s about meeting them where they are. When we give them stories they care about, let them grow at their own pace, and cheer them on; no matter what they’re reading, amazing things can happen. With patience, encouragement, and a little creativity, we can help the next generation fall in love with books—and stay in love for life.

References

Allington,R.L.(2012).What really matters for struggling readers: Designing research-based programs (3rd ed.). Pearson.

Allington, R. L., & Gabriel, R. E. (2012). Every child, every day. Educational Leadership, 69(6), 10–15. Applegate, A.J.,& Applegate, M.D.(2010). Motivating reading comprehension: Concept-oriented reading instruction. Guilford Press.

Bishop, R.S.(1990).Mirrors, windows, and sliding glass doors. Perspectives: Choosing and Using Books for the Classroom, 6(3), ix–xi.

Galda, L., Cullinan, B.E.,&Sipe,L.R.(2010). Literature and the child (8thed.).Wadsworth.

Guthrie, J.T., &Wigfield, A.(2000). Engagement and motivation in reading. Handbook of Reading Research, 3, 403–422.

Ivey, G.,& Johnston, P.H.(2013).Engaged reading as a collaborative competence. Journal of Literacy Research, 45(4), 433–454.

Lukens, R.J., Smith, J.M., & Coffel, C.M.(2013).  A critical handbook of children’s literature (9thed.). Pearson.

Miller, D.(2009). The book whisperer: Awakening the inner reader in every child. Jossey-Bass.

Nikolajeva, M.(2014). Reading for learning: Cognitive approaches to children’s literature. John Benjamins Publishing.

Rideout, V.(2017). The CommonSense Census: Media use by kids age zero to eight. CommonSense Media. Short, K. G. (2011). Literature as a way of knowing. Language Arts, 89(1), 9–16.

Trelease, J.(2013). The read-aloud handbook (7thed.). Penguin Books.

Tunnell, M.O., & Jacobs, J.S. (2017).Children’s literature, briefly (7thed.).Pearson.

Govinda Menaka is a librarian at Udgam School for Children, Thaltej. Passionate about fostering a love for reading among young learners, she is dedicated to curating inclusive and engaging library experiences. Her interests include children’s literature, literacy development, digital literacy, and promoting independent learning through creative library programs and safe reading spaces. She can be reached at menaka2494@gmail.com
item.excerpt:encoded:
Govinda Menaka
In this age of screens, small and big, how do we get our children start and stick with reading?

item.title: Start with a hook: the art of introduction in concept-based learning
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Vattam Kalpana

How do you transform a seemingly abstract mathematical concept into an exciting and memorable experience for primary and middle schoolers? The answer lies not in the blackboard, but in the buzz of a classroom solving a riddle — a puzzle wrapped in mystery, numbers, and just a hint of your own birthday.

The icebreaker that sparked inquiry

As I entered my classroom, I was committed to doing things differently from the "chalk and talk" method. I aimed not only to present the idea but also to provide a platform for one of the fundamental mathematical concepts: prime numbers. Therefore, rather than a straightforward approach, I gave my students a challenge:

“My birth date is a two-digit number, less than 30 but more than 25. The sum of its digits is a prime number. Can you guess it?

The students were immediately captivated. Giggles, hushed computations, and willing hands filled the air. The problem became a group effort, creating not only numbers, but also friendships in the classroom. Curiosity overcame apprehension.

Learning by discovery: prime numbers demystified

After cracking the birth date, I moved on to discussing prime numbers — those intriguing numbers that are only divisible by one and themselves. We then played a game:

"If your birth date is a prime number, stand up! But don't tell us — give us a riddle, a clue, or a historical connection that allows us to figure it out."

And then there was a lovely moment of bonding. One eager student jumped up and declared:

"My birthday is on Teacher's Day!"

The whole class answered in unison— September 5th! It provided a space for interdisciplinary reflection. We stopped to reflect on Dr. Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan and the principles of education, respect, and contribution. This served to create an emotional connection with the lesson and introduce a national and cultural context to numbers. It was no longer merely math — it was life combined with learning.

A mnemonic to remember: PRIME = Product of One, Myself

In order to aid in grounding the idea, I offered a light-hearted mnemonic:

PR – Product of

I – One

ME – Myself

This sentence captured the defining characteristic of prime numbers — that they are independent and not divisible by anything else other than 1 and themselves. It also triggered reflection in the students: isn't it great that we too are one-of-a-kind?

This lesson was not merely about figures. It complemented perfectly our school's teaching, learning, and lesson planning policy, which prioritized:

*Engagement through inquiry

*Inclusivity and differentiated learning

*Reflection and link to real-life contexts

*Cross-curricular links — from maths, to history, and values education

Puzzles enable students to build knowledge, not merely take it in. They provide a level-playing field in which all learners — no matter their speed or style — have a say. And above all, they promote enjoyment of learning.

Vattam Kalpana is Deputy Head for the primary school at Suchitra Academy, Hyderabad. She can be reached at kalpana.vattam@suchitra.in
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vattam Kalpana
Mathematical concepts are abstract and therefore difficult for children to understand, which is why it is important that we catch their attention and interest first.

item.title: Where learning meets play
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
S Anisha Dhinesh Kumar

Let’s face it — in today’s digital-first world, old-school lectures and chalkboards just aren’t cutting it anymore. Students want more than notes and PowerPoints — they want interaction, engagement, and a little bit of fun along the way. That’s where gamification steps in.

Simply put, gamification is the art of adding game-like elements — think points, badges, challenges, or competitions — to the learning process. And it’s not just about fun. Studies show it can boost motivation, improve focus, and make lessons more memorable.

So, how do we bring that magic into the classroom? Here are some of the most popular gamification tools that are making learning exciting in educational institutions around the world:

1. Kahoot! – learning with a buzz

A favourite among students and teachers alike, Kahoot! is a fast-paced quiz game where everyone gets to play — using their own devices.

Why students love it: Real-time questions, cool music, and that thrilling leaderboard!
Why teachers love it: It’s perfect for quick assessments and keeping everyone on their toes.
Try this: Kick off the weekend with a “Friday Kahoot!” to review the week’s lessons.

2. Quizizz – memes + MCQs = magic

If Kahoot! is a quiz show, Quizizz is the fun cousin. It lets students answer questions at their own pace and rewards them with hilarious memes along the way.

Why students love it: It’s relaxed, meme-filled, and doesn’t feel like a test.
Why teachers love it: Great for homework or low-stress classroom activities.

3. Classcraft – turning learning into an adventure

Imagine turning your class into a fantasy role-playing game. With Classcraft, students become heroes who earn points for participation, collaboration, and completing assignments.

Why students love it: They feel like they’re part of a real quest.
Why teachers love it: It encourages good behaviour, teamwork, and goal setting.
Best for: Group projects and boosting classroom dynamics.

4. Blooket – games that teach

Blooket takes learning and mixes it with fun arcade-style games. Students answer questions to progress in mini-games like racing or tower defence.

Why it works: It feels like a game but sneaks in revision and repetition.
Use it for: Competitive reviews, exam prep, or just to make Mondays more fun!

5. Flip (formerly Flip grid) – show what you know on camera

With Flip, students respond to topics by recording short, creative videos. It’s a brilliant tool for presentations, reflections, or even peer feedback.

Why students love it: It's social, personal, and feels familiar — like a school-safe TikTok.
Why educators love it: Great for boosting communication skills and student voice.

6. Breakout EDU – escape room meets education

This one’s for puzzle lovers! Breakout EDU turns your class into a live-action escape room where students work together to solve academic challenges and “break out.”

Why students love it: It’s hands-on, high-energy, and seriously fun.
Why teachers love it: Perfect for review days or building problem-solving skills.

Why try gamification in your class?

  • It grabs attention. Interactive tasks break the monotony and keep students focused.
  • It builds community. Many tools foster teamwork and healthy competition.
  • It supports deeper learning. Game-based repetition helps information stick.
  • It inspires confidence. Earning points and rewards builds positive reinforcement.

Final thoughts

Gamification isn’t just a trend or a buzzword. It’s a way to rethink the learning experience — to make it more student-centered, engaging, and effective.

Whether you’re a seasoned educator or just getting started, adding a little gamification can go a long way in creating a classroom that students actually look forward to.

So why not give it a try? Mix in some quizzes, level-ups, badges, or a little friendly rivalry — and watch your classroom come to life.

S Anisha Dhinesh Kumar is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Computer Science at Jeppiaar College of Arts and Science, where she teaches a range of subjects across BCA and BSc (CS) programs. With a strong academic background, she holds an M.Phil. in Computer Science from the University of Madras and a Master’s degree in Information Technology, complemented by a Bachelor’s degree in Software Application. Anisha has demonstrated excellence throughout her academic journey, earning university ranks, merit scholarships, and top ranks in programming languages such as C++ and Java. Her research expertise lies in the application of machine learning and deep learning techniques, particularly in skin lesion detection, with multiple publications in reputed journals and presentations at national and international conferences. Beyond research, she actively contributes to academic and institutional development by mentoring student projects, participating in faculty development programs, and has held key roles such as ICT Academy Coordinator, ED Cell member, and NSDC course trainer. Additionally, Anisha serves as an exam evaluator for the University of Madras and is committed to enhancing student skills and career readiness through placement cell activities and leadership training. Her dedication to teaching, research, and community engagement marks her as a dynamic and influential educator in the field of computer science. She can be reached at anishakani014@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anisha Dhinesh Kumar
Tech-tools that aid in teaching are aplenty. If you are new and are wondering what apps can help you bring excitement in the classroom, here is a short list to help you get going.

item.title: CyberCare: building a cyber-safe future, one student at a time
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Aarav Shah

As a class of eighth graders chat excitedly over an online game, halfway across the virtual realm, a teenager unknowingly becomes the victim of phishing. In this digital era, scenes like these are increasingly common and deeply impactful. With the rapid evolution of technology, a lack of cyber awareness is leaving many young students vulnerable to online threats ranging from game addiction to cyberbullying and beyond.

Recognizing the urgent need for intervention, Himalay Mondal from Konnagar, West Bengal, decided to act. Two years ago, while still a high school student, Himalay founded CyberCare*, a student-led voluntary organization. Born out of a personal realization about how easily young people could stray into the tangled web of cybercrimes due to sheer unawareness, CyberCare is dedicated to safeguarding students and building a resilient digital community.

"When my friend lost money to an online scam, it struck me how little we knew about protecting ourselves on the internet," recalls Himalay Mondal, founder of CyberCare. "I wanted to change that—to empower us by spreading knowledge."

CyberCare conducts widespread awareness programs directly targeting students from grades 7 to 12 across schools in India. Their efforts extend to influential community gatherings such as book fairs and science expos, using these platforms to extend the reach of their message.

Equipped with projectors and interactive audiovisual presentations, CyberCare’s approach is both engaging and informative. Using real-world examples and poignant case studies, they capture the fluctuating attention spans of young minds. From highlighting the pitfalls of in-game chats to addressing the sinister side of social media misuse, CyberCare’s seminars expose the naked truths of modern cyber threats.

"In one of our sessions, we showed how seemingly harmless in-game interactions could lead to severe privacy invasions," says Sandip Parhar, a volunteer who regularly contributes to these sessions. "The kids were shocked, but that's when learning happens."

Discussing the impact of cyber threats, CyberCare emphasizes the mental health repercussions, from anxiety to isolation, and the importance of staying informed about these dangers. The emotional weight of cyberbullying is a core topic, revealing how damaging it can be to mental health and overall wellbeing.

At its heart, CyberCare is much more than just another educational initiative. It is a grassroots movement aiming to cultivate a proactive mindset among the youth, ensuring digital resilience in a world dominated by technology. It restores confidence, encouraging youngsters to harness technology effectively rather than fearfully.

"Technology is a powerful tool, not an enemy," Himalay often concludes. "Our mission is to teach students to use it wisely and safely." Through CyberCare, Himalay and his team are not only nurturing informed digital citizens but are also sowing seeds of trust and respect between humans and technology, one classroom at a time.

* People can get in touch with CyberCare at cybercare100@gmail.com.

Aarav Shah is a high school sophomore with a strong interest in technology, cybersecurity, and digital responsibility. He serves as a media ambassador for CyberCare, helping raise awareness about online safety among youth. He can be reached at magnifed1234@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aarav Shah
Technology is all around us and youngsters these days are living in virtual worlds. When they spend most of their time with their devices, it is important that they are aware of the pitfalls too. And this is where CyberCare will help.

item.title: Writer or educator? A journey through writing in the classroom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nupur Hukmani

If you were to ask me whether I consider myself a writer or an educator first, it would be very difficult to answer. Over the years, I’ve realized that it doesn’t matter whether I am a professional writer or not. Writing is such an integral part of being a teacher – before, during, and outside the classroom.

I remember my early years as an English language teacher and my initial writing classes. Should I teach students the structure of a writing piece? Should I focus on grammar and language? How do I encourage them to write creatively, drawing from their own experiences and reflections? How do I make writing less intimidating and more engaging? I may have asked all the right questions, but when I entered the classroom, I overlooked one crucial ingredient: integration – of subjects, real-life connections, children’s reflections, and their voices.

The children were bored in my writing classes. I saw the same pattern repeat in many of the classes I observed as a teacher mentor. This was because we, as teachers, were “teaching” students how to write rather than helping them use writing as a tool to better understand themselves and the world. I also treated language as a disconnected “subject” rather than as an organic medium for expression.

On the bright side, because I’ve always been a writer, I used writing as an outlet during those difficult early years – venting frustrations and culture shock onto tear-stained pages. Today, those entries might even be entertaining to read.

Over the years, in various roles – from teaching, to mentoring, to leading – I’ve observed countless classrooms and engaged in reflective conversations with many teachers. Especially in language classes, writing is often “taught” as a specific form or skill. For example, when teaching personal narratives, some elementary teachers simply assign a topic and dictate the content to students. Sometimes, no discussion takes place in the classroom, or if it does, nothing from the discussion is reflected in the final writing piece.

In non-language subjects, writing is often reduced to a medium of recall – a way to present learned knowledge. Creativity, vocabulary, grammar, and syntax are typically taught in a piecemeal fashion by the language teacher. Unfortunately, few teachers view writing as a powerful method to drive creativity, enhance language, build executive functioning, and integrate various subjects – not just for the student, but for the teacher as well. They barely write their own pieces to use as a starting point in the classroom to drive discussions, debates, knowledge integration, and interactions to deepen understanding. That is why many times, students are bored in classrooms where they have to engage with material written in a different part of the world, material that maybe non-contextual and makes no immediate sense in their world.

Thankfully, I’ve also seen many teachers who use writing in transformative ways – through diary entries, essays, poems, checklists, lab reports, position papers, stories, and letters. These writing pieces are often considered final artifacts of learning. But what is often ignored is the journey of creating that artifact and how writing becomes an essential part of that process for both teacher and student.

This led me to explore further: What is the place of writing in education for both teacher and student? How does a teacher’s attitude toward her own writing affect student outcomes? How does a holistic view of writing impact student engagement while also fostering self-awareness, emotional regulation, and social responsibility? Can it help students develop executive skills like communication, collaboration, planning, and organization? Can it help them see that subjects are not isolated pieces of knowledge crammed into a timetable, but an integrated and meaningful way to understand and navigate the world?

I spoke to seven teachers about writing. Most of them identified as voracious readers and recognized reading as vital to improving their pedagogy. Yet, despite writing being central to their teaching practice, many did not view themselves as writers. On probing, they shared that writing helped them research, plan, organize lessons, and reflect on their teaching practices. All of them acknowledged writing as essential to communication in their respective subjects. Many agreed it helps students clarify thoughts, reflect on emotions, improve recall, and strengthen language. Unanimously, they echoed the truth about reading and writing: the more you do it, the better you get.

They also shared inspiring practices that integrate their own writing with students’ development as reflective writers.

One language teacher spoke about using journals as a powerful pedagogical tool. Her students wrote about their feelings, created mood boards, wrote letters to peers and the teacher, wrote book reviews, chronicled their days, made checklists, and created artwork related to their entries. They also responded to prompts linked to real-world issues – e.g., “Write about a news item you read – how did it make you feel? Have you seen something like this around you? What can you do about it?”

To demystify writing, she shared her own work, including a poem about her experience with harassment. This sparked deep conversations around bullying and gender inequality and led to a discussion on how “loitering is acceptable for boys but not girls”. “Writing also made my classroom more democratic,” she said. “Students gave suggestions, wrote letters of protest, and expressed emotions, which I then addressed at the classroom level.” To improve language outcomes, she used a five-part writing workshop – Ideate, Draft, Enhance, Edit, Publish (IDEEP) – to build writing skills and modelled the process alongside her students.

A geography and economics teacher shared how she created her own monthly budget during an economics lesson to contextualize the concept for her students. She prompted them with reflective questions: Is your family able to save money every month? Why or why not? What steps can you take to save more? She also shared her personal responses. As a result, one student created a family budget, started learning about the stock market, and even began a monthly SIP.

A science teacher introduced nature journals that she has been keeping for a while. Students collected, drew, and wrote about interesting aspects of the natural world. They researched their entries and shared them during science circles, alongside the teacher, who often added to her journal during breaks. She mentioned that they had managed to document a lot of birds, insects, plants, trees in their surrounding, had started talking about Nature and conservation due to this documentation and are now more attuned to their natural surroundings. Her students reported spending more time in public parks over the weekend over spending time mindlessly in a mall as they would do earlier. More importantly, they are hungry to understand Nature, our relation with it, our destruction of it, and how our life impacts so many things in our ecosystem.

I also wanted to explore how writing affects teaching practice. Many teachers I interviewed said writing is a powerful outlet for stress and reflection. Some keep personal journals, while others have formal reflection routines built into weekly check-ins. Writing, they said, allows them to process classroom experiences, refine their methods, and chart new paths forward.

An SEL (Social and Emotional Learning) arts-based teacher shared that in her personal life, writing helps her pause, reflect, and stay grounded. She mentioned that she has always been a writer, using personal journals, poetry, and plays to process complex emotions and experiences. Putting words on paper helps her feel in control.

As an educator, she conceptualized a project called Sunoh, where she collaborated with students to build emotional literacy through music – especially rap. It was an integration of writing and music through guided prompts, poetry exercises, zine-making, and storyboarding, eventually leading to a collaborative rap song. She noted that it was often a challenge to get students interested in writing in an arts-based classroom.

“They would rather draw than share verbally since writing felt intimidating – often tied to experiences of correction and judgment. But when you introduce it as a personal creative practice, and reduce the pressure by blending it with art, storytelling, and performance, it creates space for everyone – even introverts who might prefer writing.”

When asked about the impact on daily classroom culture and student experience, she said students slowly grew more confident, developed their writing skills, and began to take pride in their unique voices. Writing gave students a safe and contained way to look at emotions objectively, name feelings, and recognize patterns and behaviours – experiences they might otherwise not be able to talk about. This led to SEL outcomes such as increased empathy, self-esteem, pride in one’s identity, and stronger student-to-student connection and collaboration.

When asked about specific structures she used to lead to these outcomes, she shared several useful strategies: sentence starters, blending visual arts with writing – such as creating comic strips or zines to first visualize and then articulate thoughts; and reflection journals for conflict resolution using guided worksheets. These prompts helped students step back, think about what happened, regulate their emotions, and build a vocabulary for their feelings and experiences. She said something that struck me deeply:

“Writing in the classroom is less about correctness and more about voice.”

Another teacher I spoke to had launched a film club called Hum Dekhenge as a safe space for student expression using the mediums of cinema and writing. A writer in his leisure time, he believes writing is a powerful tool for expression and reflection, which naturally connects with films. Beyond the film club, he also uses writing in his science classroom to check for understanding and increase participation.

The film club is a monthly activity that the students love. Each month, the teacher selects a film and organizes a screening, followed by a creative writing session. After watching the film, students respond to writing prompts on themes like social justice, peace, and gender. For example, after watching Laapataa Ladies, students were asked to interview their mothers to better understand them as individuals. This opened up deep conversations about gender equality, seeing parents as people, and strengthening the parent-child connection.

He believes writing is one of the most accessible and effective ways to improve SEL outcomes. Through writing, students explore what it feels like to sit silently with themselves – it pushes them to reflect and engage in internal dialogue. In his sharing circles, where he also shares his own writing, he has seen students become emotional because they connected with what a peer shared. This led to increased acceptance and tolerance. He has observed students letting go of shame, growing more confident in expressing themselves, and realizing they’re not alone in their feeling and experience.

This teacher has tackled complex themes such as social justice, caste, gender, environment, and indigenous rights – at personal, community, national, and global levels – and managed to engage students in a spectacularly meaningful way.

So what has been the impact of these wholesome classroom innovations?

Most of the teachers I spoke to are reflective writers who share their work with students to lead a unique pedagogy in the classroom that is contextual and immediately engages students. In my opinion, this practice enriches student-teacher relationships and helps shape a classroom culture that is transparent, joyful, and accepting. Unfortunately, such practices are still not common in our education system. Writing continues to be expected from students, even though many teachers are not regular writers themselves.

The onus must be on school systems to provide autonomy, support, and space to promote writing for both students and teachers. One teacher mentioned that in her school, all teachers are required to keep a reflection journal with questions like, “What were my students’ responses and behaviours today?” and “What were my actions in response to those behaviours?” This journal also serves as a great starting point for her weekly check-ins with the school leadership.

Writing serves both as a process and an outcome of integrating SEL into teaching and learning. Regardless of the subject, writing about a topic – thinking it through, presenting it, and publishing it requires the synthesis of knowledge systems, personal beliefs, and social attitudes. It becomes a journey of unlearning and discovery for both students and teachers.

Note: I interviewed seven teachers for this article. One of them works as an arts based SEL educator in Pune. Four of them work in a low-budget private school in Pune. Two of them work in an International School in Pune. Some of the experiences mentioned stem from my own as a teacher.

References

  • https://www.aidansevers.com/post/being-a-writing-teacher-6-reasons-why-teachers-should-write
  • https://twowritingteachers.org/2016/01/24/to-write-or-not-to-write/#:~:text=If%20you're%20not%20doing,in%20a%20very%20complex%20world.
  • https://www.researchgate.net/publication/340441521_The_Effect_of_Experiential_Learning_Models_Toward_Writing_Skills_of_Narration_Primary_School_Student
  • https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/02671522.2016.1187664

Nupur Hukmani holds a masters in psychology and has been an educator for the last 10 years. She is currently working as an Assistant School Leader at Jawahar English Medium School in Pune. When she is not teaching or writing, she loves reading, doing yoga, and long-distance running. She can be reached at nupurhukmani12@gmail.com.

Related Article

The place of teacher writing in education
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nupur Hukmani
Writing is a way of feeling, thinking, and teaching. Nupur Hukmani writes what that means – for teachers, students, and learning itself.

item.title: The place of teacher writing in education
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Amit Kohli

In teacher training, the reading material often originates from someone else. Typically, this "someone else" is an expert or scholar from a distant land. Their writings, references, contexts, and examples often feel remote and perhaps unfamiliar to teachers. Consequently, establishing a natural connection between the reading material and the trainee teacher becomes quite challenging.

Teacher trainers are not unaware of this intricate situation. While selecting content relevant to the subject, they realize that there is a significant dearth of written material based on the direct experiences of local teachers. Therefore, they are compelled to choose from what is available.

In this context, translation presents a multifaceted challenge. Since foreign content is predominantly in English, understanding it in the regional language often feels like navigating through rough waters. Clearly, in such circumstances, translating the original material becomes essential. The first challenge here is to find people who can translate from English into regional languages in a fluent and high-quality manner. Moreover, their availability is not always guaranteed due to the high demand for skilled translators.

I have observed that translations sometimes deviate significantly from the original text. This divergence can hinder the reader's comprehension. After all, we all know that every language carries its own cultural context. While a literal translation of a statement may be straightforward, capturing the implied meanings, the unsaid layers, and its local nuances is not always achievable. These aspects go beyond mere linguistic expression. The task of translation involves more than just language – it encompasses the emotions that language evokes. The true essence lies not only in the words but also in the unspoken layers that cannot be neatly articulated. It's a delicate dance that transcends mere linguistic boundaries.

Dimensions of experiential writing

Both teachers and learners encounter various challenges with experiential accounts. In many situations, a dearth of local writing is often evident. If teachers begin documenting their experiences, this gap can be significantly bridged. Competent teachers can present their experiential writing in alignment with educational principles. Alternatively, in regional languages – which serve as the medium of instruction in specific regions – teachers can use examples, applications, or practical aspects of experiential writing to explain theoretical concepts. This interplay between theoretical and experiential writing can serve as a valuable medium for teacher education.

Experiential writing encompasses three dimensions: practicality, sociability, and locality. These intertwined dimensions set experiential writing apart from other academic forms of writing.[1] The practical dimension is particularly crucial because teachers are integral to the lived experiences of education. By sharing their experiences, teachers provide a tangible perspective that transcends the abstract realms of academia. Since these experiences are inherently connected to social and local interactions, experiential writing can effectively bridge the gap between materiality and immateriality during teacher training.

The utility of experiential writing

During or after teacher training, contemplating experiential writing opens up new horizons for both teachers and trainees. It can be viewed from three perspectives. First, the theoretical insight provided by the author-teacher, which offers hope to the reader-teacher. Second, the reverberations of one's own work and experiences, allowing the reader-teacher to see their own image reflected in the writing. Third, the realm of possibilities. These three dimensions converge in experiential writing provide a path for continuous development among teachers. While reading such experiential writing, teacher-readers begin to feel, "What I haven't done so far can still be accomplished." In this way, experiential writing paves a path for continuous development among teachers.[2]

Foundation of knowledge construction

In her article, Ainee Laura Forsyth Moore states[3], "Personal and professional experiential writing provide us with the 'framework of knowledge,' within which we construct knowledge and meaning." In this way, experiential writing also inspires teacher-authors to reflect on their own experiences and view them as a form of knowledge, aiding them in becoming better educators. She further adds, "My writing practice has transformed personal knowledge into a form that I can utilize in my professional work as a teacher."[4]

Renowned psychologist Jerome Bruner asserts, “Through experiences and their expression, we create an image of ourselves, constantly shaping and evolving.” He also emphasizes that sharing our experiences and narrating our stories is essentially a way of thinking and preserving human experiences.[5] Another psychological perspective by Fawcus states, "Through experiential narratives, we construct the meanings and understanding of our everyday experiences, and this process occurs through social interactions." She continues, "When we share our experiences with others, we reinterpret those experiences, reevaluate them, and also reconstruct them."[6] According to her, "Experiential narratives (or writings) go beyond the common interpretation of events and create a shared reality. By narrating our life stories, we convey who we are and our perspective on the world. It's not just about what happened; it's about how and why that event occurred, what we thought and felt about it, and what significance it holds for us."[7]

In the context of second language education, Pavlenko emphasizes that experiential writing serves as a unique source of information about inspiration, experience, struggle, defeat, and achievement.[8]

The dialogue between theory and practice

Freeman contends, "To bridge the gap (between theory and practice) and fully comprehend education, we must adopt an approach that centers around the practitioner.... Educators who seek to understand and transform education should prioritise what teachers know and how that knowledge integrates into their work."[9]

We recognize that the process of teaching and learning occurs within a complex social context.[10] Teachers' experiences can serve as a powerful medium for expressing and clarifying this complexity. These experiences assist in developing an understanding between theoretical principles of teaching and the practical process of teaching in an actual classroom.

We are aware that traditional teacher education institutions tend to emphasize theoretical principles. However, the culture of schools is steeped in practical experiences and harsh realities, which often appear distant from academic theories. When new teachers step into the classroom, they face the pressure to apply these theories, leading to a delicate balance between practical and theoretical aspects. To navigate this complexity, it becomes crucial for teacher trainees to engage with the experiences of seasoned educators. By reading about the experiences of other teachers, they gain insights that help them find their own path in the classroom.

Types of experiential writing

Experiential writing by educators can broadly be categorized into four types. Although most writings may intertwine these four categories or combine two or three of them, distinguishing their unique characteristics is not always necessary. What matters is understanding the elements within experiential writing and utilizing them effectively in relevant contexts.

Descriptive writing

In a mathematics class, the teacher is taking a lesson on division, teaching students how to share and divide equally. She poses a question to the students: "In our class, there are 42 students and 7 balls. If each ball is to be played by an equal number of students, how many groups of students can be formed?"

The students engage in discussion, ponder, ask questions, and the teacher encourages further discussion by posing more questions to the students. They discuss among themselves. Perhaps the teacher brings seven balls into the classroom and demonstrates the process of equal sharing. Following this activity, they continue discussing other questions related to dividing a number equally into various groups. In this sequence, the teacher guides the students toward the method of finding the quotient.

This example illustrates how descriptive writing based on direct experiences can be termed as "descriptive writing." It is written from the teacher's perspective, capturing what happened, as if a camera were recording the unfolding events in the classroom.

The more detailed the description or activity in this type of writing, the more useful it becomes. It allows readers to feel as if they are present in the classroom and appreciate even the smallest details that can be significant in the teaching-learning process. Descriptive experiential writing provides readers with greater freedom to independently interpret and construct meaning from classroom discussions or activities.

Reflective writing

Within the layers of the aforementioned experiences, let's explore the teacher's thought process. Imagine that the teacher, altering her writing style, records in experiential writing why she chose to maintain the same sequence of topics or questions during classroom discussions. Why she deemed it essential? What considerations led her to organize the activity involving the ball game? During the dialogue, what was running through the teacher's mind, and what might have occupied the students' thoughts? What did they each gain from this activity? How did it serve the classroom, and why?

If, alongside the detailed activity description, the teacher also includes her reflections, her own musings, and insights in the experiential writing, we can categorize it as "reflective writing." This type of writing is penned from the teacher's viewpoint. It captures the unfolding events as if a camera were recording them, but it also adds commentary.

The advantage lies in the teacher-author sharing not only the description of the activity but also her agency, perspective, and individuality with the reader-teacher. It allows the teacher to convey the authenticity, planning, outcomes, and her understanding of the entire activity. By doing so, the reader-teacher gains a deeper comprehension of the activity's holistic nature. However, occasionally, this freedom to interpret and construct meaning independently may pose challenges for the reader-teacher.

Identifying challenges

If a teacher recognizes that understanding the method of finding quotients poses difficulties for students in standards 3 and 4, it's not just an issue within her own classroom. Similar challenges likely exist in other schools, districts, and regions where students grapple with the same concept. Therefore, she documents the classroom discussions, question-and-answer sessions, incorrect responses from students, and other relevant details related to the method of finding quotients. She frames this issue as a broader problem in mathematics education at the elementary level, which we can term as "identifying challenges."

While it's preferable to pinpoint the specific issue – such as the method of finding quotients – in this type of writing, the teacher's extensive description of her efforts and the students' responses enhances the effectiveness of the writing. Identifying challenges becomes crucial because we observe that many teachers wrestle with various questions and problems in their classrooms, often assuming that these issues are unique to their context. Sometimes, they form the misconception that as educators, they are unsuccessful in assisting their students or that the students themselves lack understanding.

When such teachers share their experiences by highlighting a problem in their experiential writing, they not only present their classroom-specific issue as a broader concern in school education but also invite solutions from fellow educators worldwide. Perhaps their problem could inspire new research or innovative approaches. In this way, they contribute not only to their own students but also to the entire educational community.

Problem-solving

When discussing any concept in the classroom, such as the method of finding quotients, there can be multiple ways to facilitate learning. Numerous teachers, based on their experiences, understanding, and the pace of learning and comprehension among their students, employ different activities. Often, what one teacher discusses, questions, or conducts in her classroom can serve as an exemplary model for other educators. If another teacher is grappling with a similar problem, the innovative approach adopted by one teacher to present a specific concept in the classroom, while documenting her classroom experience in detail, can fall under the category of "problem-solving."

For instance, let's consider the method of finding quotients. Imagine that a teacher meticulously records not only the oral or written steps of dividing objects equally into groups but also the variations of this process – repeated subtraction, addition, or multiplication. This comprehensive approach to teaching quotient calculation could serve as a model, an illustrative example, or an innovative technique that other teachers can adapt in their classrooms.

In the end, we can assert that teachers should continue writing about their experiences and sharing them within the teaching community. Ideally, this writing should be in the teacher's mother tongue or regional language – the medium of instruction in their respective regions. By doing so, they connect their work with educational principles and assist fellow teachers. It would be beneficial if this writing begins with private diary entries.

[1]      Conoley, J. C., & Clandinin, D. J. (2006). Handbook of complementary methods in educational research.

[2]      Green, M. (2008). From Bear Facts to Intellectual Possibility: The Leap of Imagination.

[3]      Maitos, A. M. (Ed.). (2009). Narrative Frameworks of Living, Learning, Researching, and Teaching: Narratives on Teaching and Teacher Education.

[4]      op. cit.

[5]      Bruner, J. (2002). Making stories: Law, literature, life. Farrar, Straus and Giroux.

[6]      Fawcus, R. (2006). Memory and Narrative, Self and Voice.

[7]      op. cit.

[8]      Pavlenko, A. (2001). Applied Linguistics 2.

[9]      Freeman, D. (1996). Voices from the Language Classroom. K. Bailey & D. Nunan (Eds.).

[10]     Vygotsky, L. S. (1996). Mind in Society: The Development of Higher Psychological Processes.

Amit Kohli, with over 20 years of experience in education, community development, and social impact, works with Lok Biradari Prakalp, Hemalkasa. A published author and versatile educator, he specializes in teacher training, curriculum design, and inclusive education, inspiring holistic growth through his innovative approaches. A keen photographer and lifelong learner, Amit’s work continues to inspire growth and accessibility in education. He can be reached at amt1205@gmail.com.

Related Article

Writer or educator? A journey through writing in the classroom
item.excerpt:encoded:
Amit Kohli
What happens when teachers write their own stories? Amit Kohli writes about the different kinds of experiential writing that can reshape teacher training in India.

item.title: Math meets creativity: a RAFT journey
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Thonisha Joselin Sonia T

As a math teacher, my goal is to create a vibrant learning environment that nurtures my students’ creativity while deepening their understanding of mathematical concepts. One strategy that has proven effective in achieving this is the RAFT (Role, Audience, Format, Topic) approach. This engaging method allows students to explore math concepts from different perspectives and express their learning in innovative ways.

The RAFT activity: a creative twist in math

One of my favourite RAFT activities was designed to teach the concept of square roots. This activity not only helped students grasp the relationship between whole numbers and their square roots but also encouraged them to express their understanding creatively.

The RAFT assignment:

Role: square root

Audience: whole numbers

Format: love letter

Topic: explain the relationship

For this task, the students stepped into the shoes of a square root. They wrote heartfelt love letters to their corresponding whole numbers, beautifully articulating the unique bond between them.

An example:

Dear 4,

I hope this letter finds you well. As your devoted square root, I wanted to take a moment to express how special our relationship is. Every time I see you, I am filled with admiration for the perfection you represent.

When I think of you, I see myself as a smaller version of who you are. You are 4 and I am 2. Together, we create something beautiful, as when I multiply myself by myself (2 x 2), I am proud to produce you.

You are not just a number to me; you embody stability and balance in the world of mathematics. Whenever anyone asks about square roots, I eagerly mention you because you exemplify how we are intricately connected.

I cherish the times when you allow me to shine, showing others how I can reveal your true essence. Together, we teach the importance of understanding and embracing relationships, whether in math or in life.

With all my love,

Your square root, 2

A learning experience beyond math

This activity provided several benefits that extended beyond the realm of mathematics:

1. Enhanced understanding: Writing from the square root's perspective deepened students' comprehension of the relationship between numbers and their roots.

2. Creative expression: Students loved the opportunity to write creatively, making their learning experience enjoyable and memorable.

3. Language development: While focusing on math, students also honed their English writing skills, expressing their thoughts with clarity and emotion.

4. Engagement and motivation: The unconventional format of a love letter sparked interest and enthusiasm among students, motivating them to participate actively.

5. Collaboration and critical thinking: Sharing their letters with classmates led to collaborative discussions, enabling students to learn from each other’s ideas.

Extending creativity with student-led RAFTs

After completing this activity, I encouraged students to design their own RAFT assignments, selecting topics such as fractions, exponents, and whole numbers. They explored diverse formats like poems, sick notes, and leave letters. Their creativity was wonderful to see!

For instance: One student wrote a sick note from a numerator explaining how its denominator wasn’t reducing fractions properly. Another penned a poem from an exponent to a base number, celebrating their power together.

The best part was the joy of reading these write-ups and watching students present their ideas in class. This exercise not only nurtured their creativity but also inspired critical thinking as they found unique ways to explain complex concepts.

Conclusion

The RAFT strategy has truly transformed my math classroom into a space where creativity thrives alongside learning. Through activities like writing love letters and crafting their own RAFTs, students discovered new ways to understand math while enhancing their language skills. By blending creativity with critical thinking, the RAFT approach has made math relatable, engaging, and fun for my students.

This journey of learning and innovation has reinforced my belief that math is not just about numbers – it’s about stories, relationships, and endless possibilities!

Thonisha Joselin Sonia is TGT Math, SRV Senior Secondary Public School, Trichy. She can be reached at thonishajoselinsonia@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Thonisha Joselin Sonia T
Can you imagine poems, sick notes, and letters in math class? Thonisha Joselin Sonia T writes about a math class where imagination counts as much as calculation.

item.title: Writing as a stepping stone
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

This is the story of two teachers from two different places: a fifth grade Hindi teacher and a second grade EVS teacher – and how they came together in a unique way.

Both belong to an online community of teachers that has been meeting regularly for over four years now, and have even carried out action research on several topics. They ascribe much of their vibrancy and brimming ideas to this enriching community.

As they read research papers for their action research, one paper particularly caught the attention of the EVS teacher as it proposed ways in which students could write about real-life situations.

She shared this idea excitedly with her second graders, but was surprised by the response of one seven-year-old girl: “Why should I write when there is so much technology today to do it for me?” This was familiar to the Hindi teacher too, as she had experienced a good deal of resistance from her students to write in Hindi, try as she might to infuse the writing with zing – like having them write to the district magistrate or the prime minister, etc.

“Children just don’t want to write these days!” both teachers bemoaned.

The urge to develop their students’ writing skills was germinating within both teachers. Since the Hindi teacher was teaching the fifth grade and the EVS teacher was now being asked to handle the fourth and fifth grades, both of them brainstormed on ways to get their students to cultivate the habit of writing. It almost seemed like three pieces of a jigsaw puzzle fit snugly into place: the idea from the research paper, their friendship, and the need to get their students to write.

Like a 1000 watt bulb, an idea flashed in both their minds: “Why not get their students to write letters to each other? Across the two locations?”

And this set the ball rolling – into a cascade of events that had a totally unexpected culmination! Starting with an excited telephone call late at night, the two collaborators began creating a novel experience both for themselves as well as their students. Their description of the way children learnt to write meaningfully deserves to be heard (the link to the video is at the end of this article). One school used the opportunity to show how writing can be crafted and edited to fine tune it, while the other kept the original tone and language of the students by keeping editing out of the plan.

As children of one school began to get to know their friends from the other school, they felt like meeting them physically. What started off as kindling the love for writing in children (and helping them see its value even in this age of AI) now turned into something larger: children could get exposed to their counterparts in a different state, learn about the ways of that school and community and forge deeper links with their pen friends, if they met in person. Pretty soon, the children owned the idea of visiting the other school, and became the advocates of the plan as they convinced their parents. So too did other teachers in the two schools: as they got infected by the enthusiasm of the protagonists, and soon saw value in this initiative. The exchange of letters had kindled the curiosity of the children about different personalities, and this could only be quenched when they met in person.

Both teachers now had to work hard to convince their respective school managements of the value of a visit to the other school. Donning the role of administrators was new to both, but their drive to see this happen propelled them. Naturally, since this required other teachers of their respective schools to pitch in, this duo worked systematically to break the program into steps, so as to avoid overwhelming other teachers who had not participated at the ideation stage. I was struck by their cognizance of dropping the assumption that everyone would share their enthusiasm about their idea.

Who would have thought that an intent to develop writing skills in children would end up like this?

Click on the link below to know how the exchange program flowered https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uFuZLsd-G4g

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
Few things are required to make writing exciting again: One simple idea and two passionate teachers. Read Neeraja Raghavan’s story on pen pals and possibilities.

item.title: The mystery of the moving Sun
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Alekhya Bellapu

Do you remember ever wondering why the Sun seems to rise in the morning and set in the evening?

Imagine a classroom buzzing with excitement as young minds embark on a journey to understand the Sun's movements. The idea for this activity stemmed from a simple conversation with a colleague; we discussed activities to better understand the Earth's rotation and its impact on the Sun's apparent movement. Inspired by this discussion, I planned an interactive session to capture the children's attention and integrate various subjects seamlessly.

Sparking curiosity with a sundial

Instead of a traditional lecture, I made a giant sundial using readily available materials: chart paper, cardboard, and a stick. The moment I revealed the sundial, the classroom buzzed with excitement. "Is it a clock?" one student asked. "Are we going to learn about time?" another chimed in. Their curiosity was officially piqued – the perfect starting point for our exploration.

From observations to understanding

Students observed the stick casting a shadow on the chart paper. Savarni, a bright young scholar, recalled her science lessons and confidently explained how opaque objects like the stick block light, and therefore cast their shadows. This observation set the foundation for deeper understanding.

Connecting the dots: Sun, shadows, and time

Throughout the day, the class kept revisiting the sundial. They observed how the shadow's position changed with time, marking morning, midday, and evening on the chart paper. Nainika, demonstrating exceptional mathematical skills, emphasized writing "9 a.m." for morning and "4:00 p.m." for evening.

Beyond the sundial: a lesson in teamwork and perspective

The learning extended beyond the sundial. Students stood on a large circle marked on the ground. Their shadows fell at different points as the Sun moved across the sky. It took three people to form a single shadow in the morning, compared to seven in the afternoon. This activity fostered teamwork and observation skills, allowing them to compare shadow lengths and relate them to the time of day.

The Earth takes the stage

To test my students, I suggested that the Sun was moving across the sky. But my clever students, armed with knowledge from other subjects, were quick to correct me! They confidently explained concepts like gravity and the solar system. To solidify their understanding, they even used the analogy of a merry-go-round – we feel the world moves while riding a merry-go-round, but it's actually the merry-go-round (Earth) that's rotating!

The learning continues: measuring and comparing

The final lesson involved measuring shadow lengths. Students discovered that evening shadows were the longest, further reinforcing their grasp of the Sun's changing position throughout the day.

A day filled with discovery

This engaging activity allowed the students to actively participate in their learning. They observed, questioned, discussed, and made connections between the movement of the Sun, shadows, time, and different parts of the day.

Beyond the classroom walls

This "Mystery of the Moving Sun" activity is a testament to the power of inquiry-based learning. By using simple, readily available materials and transforming the classroom into a playground for discovery, we sparked a love for science and exploration in these young minds.

The points I kept in mind for this lesson

Let curiosity lead the way: Embrace inquiry-based learning and let the children's curiosity guide the exploration.

A symphony of subjects: Integrate English, mathematics, and global perspectives for a holistic learning experience, especially in the early years of learning.

Simple yet effective tools: Utilize readily available materials to enhance learning.

Embrace collaboration: Encourage teamwork and discussion.

Challenge and celebrate: Use playful challenges to question preconceived notions and celebrate discoveries.

Alekhya Bellapu is an academic leader and curious educator who enjoys watching questions unfold into discoveries. With a background in both CBSE and Cambridge curricula, she believes in hands-on, inquiry-led learning, where every moment sparks wonder, every conversation builds perspective, and every child becomes a seeker of the truth. She can be reached at alekhya.bellapu@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Alekhya Bellapu
Students unravel the mystery of the Sun’s movement with a homemade sundial. With their curiosity engaged, they learn about science and teamwork, writes Alekhya Bellapu.

item.title: Rocky residents
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Kobita Das Kolli

Early monsoon skies of tumultuous grey clouds and sweeping blue washes of rain captivate us as we scramble and trek up fantastic piles of boulders seeking the wide unfolding vistas of an expanding city or the glowing drama of the setting sun. But even as we enjoy this experience, how many of us actually look down and see the wave-like concentric patterns of teal adorning the boulders (lichens), or the spidery stalks of pink nestled amid the green in shallow rock pans (Ledebouria hyderabadensis), or the carpets of white stars (Euploca strigosa)?

The 2.5 billion-year-old Archean granitic outcrops of Hyderabad are older than the Himalayas. As part of the Indian plate, they travelled away from the African continental mass towards Eurasia, upon the breakup of Gondwana, around 140 million years ago. Millennia of weathering created the marvellous formations we see today. Being in the hinterland, semi-arid Hyderabad has experienced stable conditions without any major landscape altering effects of floods, landslides, or earthquakes. Hence, organisms have adapted and evolved to gradually establish and occupy various niches of the rocky outcrops to form life sustaining habitats.

How do plants colonize bare rock?

Among the first pioneers on bare rock are crustose lichens. Minerals from the rock, sunlight, and moisture in the atmosphere are sufficient for them to grow and survive, and in doing so lichens weather rock slowly to form substrate and add organic debris as they decompose. Other early settlers such as liverworts and mosses arrive as spores to colonize the substrates thus formed. The conditions on bare rock are like those that existed when plants like liverworts first colonized land 470 million years ago. With an ability to survive dry conditions as spores, liverworts and mosses grow only during moist periods because they possess simple, water absorbing structures which absorb moisture directly from their surroundings. Both liverworts and mosses dry out to form biocrusts in dry periods. The biocrust mats grow in thickness by accumulating and holding organic debris and weathered rock. Travelling spore seeds may find suitable conditions to colonize the area, and thus the process continues.

Adaptations help plants grow and survive

Life on rocks experience temperature extremes, sometimes as much as 20 degrees in a single day. Sunlight is plentiful but can be damaging. Seasonality means moisture is available only during the monsoon, June to October, followed by cool dry months, November to February, and hot dry months from March to May. Wind causes drying or desiccation even as moisture is scarce. The gravelly soil is poor, lacking in depth and nutrients, more so when water seeps and flows continuously over a gradient, washing away precious soluble nutrient.

Herbaceous plant species, which need moisture to grow, display unique strategies of adaptive evolution to counter harsh conditions. Most are ephemeral but can also be desiccation tolerant, succulents, geophytes, or even carnivores. All these species grow, flower, and set seed to multiply during the short monsoon season.

Ephemerals (short-lived plants) grow quickly, flower abundantly, set seed, and die. A large number of drought tolerant seeds remain dormant until the next growing season. Thick-leaf lavender (Coleus strobilifer) is one such ephemeral plant. Thicker than normal leaves help the plant conserve water during the short dry periods during the monsoon. There is an abundance of ephemeral grass species on sandy rock soil like the Desert Black Millet (Melanocenchris jacquemontii) with its short stalks of unique flowers.

Dessication tolerant species have a remarkable example in Sanjeevini (Selaginella bryopteris), which belongs to the spore producing clubmoss family. These short proto ferns are adapted to dry completely and curl inwards to protect the central growing tip of the plant. The dried selaginella look like pebbles blending into the gravelly ground. As soon as moisture is available, they unfurl and resume life within a few hours. This magical ability to come back to life is the reason for the epithet Sanjeevini, one that infuses life. These species which can dry out but resume living in the presence of moisture are also called resurrection plants. The ability to revive itself was thought to give sanjeevini the ability to revive humans and could be the one referred to in the Ramayana, although its efficacy as a medicinal plant is not proven.

Succulent species like Caralluma (Caralluma fimbriata), have done away with leaves, thus preventing water loss through transpiration. Instead, they possess fleshy green stems which store water and can photosynthesize. Being succulent, they can exist through the dry months, losing moisture and shrinking if conditions are drier than usual.

Geophytes have storage organs like bulbs, corms, or tuberous roots, thus the plant survives underground and dormant, away from desiccating conditions. They bloom and leaf out during the monsoon season, storing reserves to tide over the dry season. A notable geophyte is Hyderabad squill (Ledebouria hyderabadensis), which was first reported from the city’s rocky outcrops. It is a small plant with a bulb, considered endemic to Hyderabad.Another charismatic geophyte which occurs here is Spiral Ceropegia which has corms as storage organs (Ceropegia spiralis).

Carnivorous plants like the Indian sundew (Drosera indica) have adapted their leaves to capture insects. Long thread-like leaves bear hairs tipped with sticky beads which glisten in the sunlight attracting insects, hence the name sundew. Once trapped, the leaf curls over the insect and releases digestive enzymes that allow the nitrogen rich nutrients to be absorbed by the plant. Nutrients thus gained supplement the nitrogen deficient marshy conditions where these plants grow.

Mature rocky habitats

So far, we have seen various adaptations of the smaller herbaceous species, which enable them to thrive on rock during the wet season and tide over the dry season with desiccation tolerant spores, seeds, and storage organs. Most of them are ephemeral, growing during favourable conditions and the majority are unseen during dormancy. From simple to higher orders, almost all groups of flora occur on rocky outcrops, algae, bryophytes (liverworts and mosses), pteridophytes (ferns), and angiosperms, except for gymnosperms. Larger herbs, shrubs, vines, climbers, and trees have managed to establish themselves as and when conditions have favoured their growth. Chasmophytes cling in crevices and cracks. Boulders sport green garlands of plants rooted in the cool, moist, humus rich substrate trapped at their bases. Aquatic plants from algae to angiosperms occur in shallow and deep rock pools, some ephemeral, others perennial. Perennial, woody, deep-rooted plants are slow growing and stunted, optimizing the paucity of resources. Leaves are variously adapted to prevent moisture loss, covered in fine hairs, thickened, or covered in a waxy cuticle. They are deciduous, adapted to surviving dry months by shedding all leaves to control water loss due to transpiration. Therefore, vegetation is present throughout the year.

In the dry season, amid the golden swathes of dry grass and grass flowers, many leafless shrubs sport berries or papery seed-pod bracts. Delicate vines will disappear but survive dormancy as tuberous rootstock, whereas leafless woody climbers display spiralling or arching sculptural forms with distinct features giving a clue to their identity. Admirable bark and branching patterns define the trees otherwise shorn of identifiable green. The ground beneath is crisp with texture and the warmth of a hundred hues of fallen leaves, clues to the trees if we choose to peruse.

Come spring and the woody perennial species awaken with a burst of flowering. Captivating colour and fragrance coupled with the chance spotting of migratory birds, those which prefer rocky outcrops and their flora, are good reasons to savour the season. Soon there will be fruit, ripening in the warming days, alluring orange, red, and purple berries. Few perennials begin to leaf shortly after flowering thus spring-green patches enliven the browns. Almost all seeds are dispersed before the hottest months of April and May, lying in wait for the life-giving south-west monsoon. There is a resurgence of life with the onset of the rains when the herbaceous ephemerals and perennials, a legion of insects and other fauna take their chances to create a new generation.

Importance of the flora of rocky outcrops

Rocky outcrops with vegetation form Small Natural Features in the landscape. They are biodiverse islands supporting a variety of flora and fauna, which together impact the surrounding landscape in ways greater than the sum of its parts. Not only do they boost local biodiversity, but they also provide ecosystem services like clean air, carbon sequestration, pollinator havens, and improve the climate and hydrology of the area. They can serve as living laboratories to showcase plant groups, study evolutionary adaptations, species interactions, and desiccation tolerance. Going forward, in this era of climate change we might just find the answers to survival from the resilient floral residents of rocky outcrops.

References

  • Aparna Watve; Rocky Outcrops as Special Habitats in North Western Ghats, Maharashtra; Jan 2008 Geosciences.
  • Porembski, S., et.al., Islands on Islands – Habitats on Inselbergs,1998, Ecological Studies.
  • Aboli Kulkarni, et.al., Indian rock outcrops: review of flowering plant diversity, adaptations, floristic composition and endemism, Oct 2022, Tropical Ecology.
  • Rockoutcropofindia.in

Kobita Das Kolli is an amateur naturalist and independent researcher documenting the flora and small fauna of rocky outcrops and mixed deciduous forests in and around Hyderabad. She shares her findings on walks, talks, and the social media. She continues to guide a nature activity class at Vidyaranya School, Hyderabad. Kobita is an illustrator and has worked at Titan as a watch designer after graduating in Plant Sciences and MPhil in Plant Physiology. She can be reached at kobi25dk@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Kobita Das Kolli
Did you know that Hyderabad’s rocks are home to abundant plant life. These species are resilient, adaptive, and wildly beautiful, writes Kobita Das Kolli.

item.title: Cloud adventures: unleashing creativity and curiosity
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Priyanka Soman

Sometimes, the most profound experiences arise from the simplest of activities. In our fast-paced lives, filled with relentless schedules, screens, and responsibilities, we often forget to pause, look up, and savour the beauty above us. Recently, my class and I embarked on a journey that required nothing more than our eyes and a vast, open sky. We participated in the enchanting practice of cloud gazing.

Cloud gazing: finding peace in the sky

Imagine lying back on a gentle patch of grass, the earth cools beneath you as you gaze into the endless sky, pure and blue, where clouds drift in and out of form like a secret language. There’s an elephant, big and lumbering, its trunk curling around a patch of sunlight; over there, a grand castle rises with tall spires before the breeze softly whisks it into something new – perhaps a dragon’s tail, long and sinuous, winding its way across the heavens. Every cloud is a new canvas, brushed by invisible hands, morphing and reshaping into scenes as vivid as daydreams. When you’re cloud gazing, it’s as if your mind floats upward, joining the clouds as they drift along, and your thoughts feel lighter, playful, filled with wonder. You close your eyes briefly, and when you look again, you spot a giant whale gliding slowly across the sky, its tail fin curling in a soft arc, leaving you with a feeling of peace that matches the gentle rhythm of the clouds.

The magic of cloud watching

It was my co-teacher who encouraged me to try this seemingly simple activity. It was amazing how the clouds seemed to come alive with shapes and colours, almost like moving sculptures in the sky. We didn’t realize how much time we spent there, just looking and imagining. And as we lay there, we could feel the gentle breeze and warmth of the sun, almost as if the whole sky was inviting us to slow down and just be present.

Cloud gazing is more than just seeing shapes; it’s about feeling connected to something bigger than ourselves. Every time you take a deep breath, it's like you’re linking yourself to that vast expanse above.
