It was as if the clouds were sharing secrets of the sky, teaching us to just let go and be in the moment.

Letting your imagination soar

When you watch the clouds, it’s like opening a book that’s different every time you read it. You could lie under a summer sky and imagine those fluffy shapes turning into gardens, or imagine that clouds could be future floating landscapes in the sky, filled with flowers and birds. It’s a perfect place to let your mind wander and create worlds that only you can see.

Patience and peace

As the clouds drifted lazily across the sky, they seemed to remind us that not everything in life needs to move quickly. We lay there, letting the stillness settle in as each cloud shifted in shape and colour. One moment, a cloud stretched long and wispy, like strands of white silk brushed against the sky. Then slowly, it softened and rolled, forming into something new – a ship, a mountain, even a quiet lion resting on the horizon. We watched, mesmerized, noticing how each change took its time, unhurried and beyond our control.

A journey back to Nature

The cloud-watching trip wasn’t just about gazing at the sky – it became a moment of rediscovery, reminding us all of the quiet wonder surrounding us. As a class, we talked about how our tech-heavy routines often pull us away from noticing the simple, unassuming beauty of Nature. It’s easy to get caught up in screens and schedules, but Nature remains constant, patient, waiting to be embraced rather than controlled.

For a while, we lay back on the grass, watching shapes form and dissolve in the clouds: a dragon drifting lazily across the blue sky, a castle slowly melting into a giant’s face. Each of us saw something different, but we shared that feeling of being small in comparison to something infinitely larger. When we finally stood up, there was a calmness, a gentleness in everyone’s movements. It was as if we’d all found a deeper connection – to Nature, to each other, and to the quiet peace that comes when you’re reminded of the beauty that’s always there, just waiting to be seen.

What clouds teach us

Clouds are like gentle teachers, reminding us of the power of silence and wonder. There’s a lot of beauty in the simple act of watching, of just being part of something without needing to understand or explain it. When we cloud gaze, we become both the observer and part of the scenery. It’s as if the clouds are saying, “Come with us, let your worries float away.”

The beauty of looking up

In a world that often rushes by at an overwhelming pace, our time spent cloud gazing offered a gentle reminder of the beauty of slowing down. It invited us to take a pause in our busy lives and to embrace a moment of tranquillity. This simple act requires no special tools or elaborate skills – only a willingness to sit quietly and let Nature unfold its wonders.

As we lay beneath the expansive sky, our cloud-gazing experience revealed profound truths about life and adventure. Sometimes, the most meaningful journeys are found in the simplest of moments. In the dance of the clouds, we discovered stories waiting to be told, dreams yearning to be born, and a deep connection to the world around us.

So, the next time you have a chance, take a moment to lie down, look up, and let the clouds carry your thoughts away. You might be surprised at the beauty and the insights that unfold when you allow yourself to simply be. In that stillness, you may find the clarity and peace you didn’t even realize you were seeking.

Priyanka Soman is an educator and writer with a keen interest in integrating the arts, sciences, and storytelling to create meaningful learning experiences. She can be reached at cspriyankaa@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Priyanka Soman
In a tech-driven world, one teacher’s class lay back on the grass and looked up to find creativity, calm, and connection, writes Priyanka Soman.

item.title: The true meaning of inclusion: my journey with Arjun
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ayna Ismail

As an educator, I often wondered what being a “special” teacher truly means. Do I need special qualifications to teach children with unique needs? Does being “special” have a distinct role in the classroom? These questions have stayed with me for a long time, but I have realized that true inclusion is not about having a special title, but about recognizing and ensuring that every child contributes to the learning experience.

A new perspective on inclusion

Arjun is different from us – not because he worries less, but because his worries are different. While we stress over work, news, and endless tasks, he seems untouched by anything. He moves at his own pace, smiling through everything, making those around him happy. But that does not mean life is easy for him. Arjun has his struggles; his own pace, sudden tantrums, and challenges that others may not see. Yet, his bright smile never fades. He is the cheerful heart of grade 4 Beta, spreading joy wherever he goes.

Arjun has autism. While the world’s chaos does not trouble him, he faces his battles every day.

I still remember my first day teaching grade 2 at Aimee International School. As a beginner, I faced many challenges that overwhelmed me. One moment stood out – Arjun hesitated to make eye contact when I asked for his name. He answered in a low voice, and at that moment, I realized he was not ready to accept me.

As he covered his ears to block out the noise, as he hummed tunes to find comfort, I watched him grow – not just in age but in resilience. Each self-soothing act made me wonder how to reach him, how to step into his world instead of pulling him into mine. Inclusion in a class of 20 was never just about making space for him; it was about understanding his rhythm, adapting to his needs, and learning that progress is not measured by how well he fits in, but by how freely he can be himself.

Over the past three years with my grade 4 Beta class, I have learned that

  1. Inclusion is about people, not just programs.
  2. Every child contributes to the classroom experience.
  3. Peer support is powerful.

We are happy to have Arjun in our class. He is surrounded by incredible peers – Nandhan and Vaibhav, who always lend him a hand; Abhinav, his confidant; Rupeksha and Moksha, his closest companions; Sarayu, his supportive bench partner, Manha, his tuition mate, and Hyma aunty (Nandan’s mom). These friendships show how a truly inclusive atmosphere is built, not by policies, but by children who support one another.

Challenges and lessons along the way

At the beginning of my journey, I doubted whether I was cut out for teaching. But through ups and downs, I learned that being firm yet kind helped me build stronger connections with my students. One student, Arjun, challenged me in ways I never expected.

Arjun was initially withdrawn, and the more I tried to connect with him, the more he pulled away. But instead of forcing change, I learned to meet Arjun where he was. This journey taught me valuable lessons:

  • Listening and patience: Instead of expecting immediate change, I gave Arjun the space he needed and focused on building trust.
    • Breaking tasks into smaller steps: Arjun found large tasks overwhelming, so I learned to break them into smaller, manageable steps. With guidance from the school’s director, Omkara sir, we used simple techniques like segmentation – having Arjun use his thumb to space out words, which helped make his writing more organized.
    • Emotional connections matter: Arjun expressed his emotions in different ways – from tearing papers to throwing tantrums. Instead of stopping him, I tried letting him express himself, which helped me understand him better.

The turning point

As we worked together, something shifted. Arjun’s view slowly changed. The breakthrough came when I started using his incredible memory to help him learn. By focusing on his strengths, he began to grasp concepts more easily, and the results were encouraging.

All thanks to his strong and supportive mother. Her unwavering belief in Arjun has been truly inspiring. Seeing how a parent’s love and dedication shape a child’s journey has been one of the most rewarding parts of my teaching experience.

This journey also taught me that every child has unique strengths. Arjun’s memory became his biggest asset, especially in Hindi. Watching him put in the effort to read, pushing past his barriers, was a moment of pure joy. Inclusion is not just about teaching but about adapting, supporting, and believing in each child’s potential.

What inclusion means

Arjun’s story is not just about his strength, but also about how we, as educators and peers, support one another in growth. He reminded me that inclusion is not a set of guidelines – it is about understanding, adapting, and believing in every child.

Through this journey, I have realized that inclusion is not a special program or qualification – it is a commitment. Every small step we take together creates a stronger, more inclusive classroom where every child feels valued and capable of success.

Ayna Ismail is a lifelong learner and lead facilitator in English at Aimee International School. She believes teaching is a shared journey of growth. Passionate about reading, travel, adventure, and poetry, Ayna blends structure with joy to create engaging, reflective learning spaces that nurture both intellect and self-love. She can be reached at aimee.ayna@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ayna Ismail
A teacher learns how inclusion is much more than a special title; it’s about adapting to the child and believing in them.

item.title: Stories – one's world, everyone's world
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Varun Gupta

"Why don't you cry anymore?" "Don't you miss your father now?" A few 9 and 10-year-olds asked their classmate. Five years ago, this girl had lost her father to COVID. Since then, the mother has been taking care of her daughter by herself. The mother considered the school a trusted partner in helping her keep her child safe, secure, and happy, both emotionally and physically. So when her daughter was subject to such questions, the mother shared the incident with us with a heavy heart. The incident shook us. While we were searching for a way to apologize to the mother, a thought came to my mind and I assured her that we teachers would talk to the children and sort things out.

I spoke to my fellow teachers about the need to sensitize children about their behaviour and we decided that stories would be a good way to initiate a conversation with them. According to the children’s age and the context of the situation, I suggested reading to the children the story Meri Zoya Chali Gayi. The protagonist in this story is a girl who has lost her sister. Along with the read-aloud, we also decided to perform a skit depicting an incident similar to the girl’s experience in school.

After we read the story and performed the skit, we asked the children what they felt. Before we took on this project, the children did not believe that they had to be sensitive and behave according to the situation; they had also thought nothing wrong in asking ‘difficult questions’. But through the story and our skit, the girl and the other children understood that death is one of the saddest events they would face, causing trauma to the family, and therefore it should be seen and treated mindfully and empathetically. Of course, we did not have too many expectations from the children from just this one session; our hope was that the girl would get support and courage and that the other children would understand the need for tenderness and become aware of their own and others' feelings.

After the session, the girl went home happy and the mother shared her gratitude with us teachers. At that point, we realized the significance of our profession and were grateful for being able to do our little bit. I shared this small success of ours with the writer of the story and thanked her for her creation. I got an immediate reply from the writer, thanking us for using her story in such a manner and remembering the contribution of the illustrator and others in the making of the book. It was then that I realized that a story can create a healthy ecosystem of education by involving children, teachers, writers, illustrators, families, school, and society. In trying to understand the girl’s world, all our worlds got connected; no one is alone, no one's world is isolated.

It is not just the popular stories that connect with children, we must also introduce them to stories that are less known but which can support them and help them express themselves.

We read one such story a few days ago – Panchhi Pyara dealing with the sorrow of losing of a grandparent. As we read the story, many children cried. They remembered and shared memorable moments they spent with their grandparents.

“Now I will not be able to be happy for some days”. This is what a 10-year-old student said when we read Sadako. There are countries that have been fighting continuously for years, and it is innocent people and children that are being killed. Far away from war, living in more comfortable circumstances, what were our children thinking? It was in this context that the story of Sadako was chosen with its themes of friendship, love, family affection, destiny, and hope, which touched the hearts of the children. As Sadako’s friends kept making colourful golden paper cranes and birds for her, our children hoped that maybe Sadako would be safe. She will start playing and running again. But by the time they reached the last pages, the children’s eyes became moist. "There should be no war at all, innocent people and children should not be hurt, at the most the heads of the countries that want to fight should fight among themselves and the rest should stay safe," they said. During this time, Shin's Tricycle was also given to the children to read. A parent said, "The story was so painful and serious that not only the children, but even I could not read it." Sometimes I do wonder if I am doing right by those children who are more sensitive than others. Will such stories do them harm? Perhaps not. Suffering is part of life and learning to deal with it is too.

I believe that the most important objective of education is to make our children capable. Perhaps with such diverse stories, we can imagine a peaceful world inhabited by educated, thoughtful, soft, and pure hearted citizens.

Note: I would like to express my gratitude to Ms Smriti Jain (cofounder) and Dr. Tapaswini Sahu (Academic Director) of IAAT (I Am A Teacher) for helping me understand that language is an art, and for the deep and vast perspectives in literature, my thanks and gratitude to Dr. Sonika Kaushik and Ms. Shivani Bajaj (mentors of Tata Trust Parag).

Varun Gupta is currently learning and teaching in a private school in Gurugram. He can be reached at varun.flexituff@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Varun Gupta
How can stories teach children to be kind? Varun Gupta writes about the power of books and stories in helping children deal with grief and reignite hope.

item.title: Federalism and Bicameralism
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: The last time we spoke, you said India is organized as a federal union, so that there is no concentration of power in the central government. This means, each state government has autonomy in many areas to govern independently. The central government comprising the legislature has oversight from multiple spaces – opposition parties within the legislature, the judiciary, media, and civil society. This is a fairly complex structure to ensure checks and balances.

Within this complex system, we also have two houses in the legislature – the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha – both of which have different methods of election. Why do we need two houses? Wouldn’t a simpler parliamentary democracy be enough? Elected representatives make decisions on behalf of the people, therefore fulfilling the general will. That way we would be closer to republicanism or direct democracy as it is called.

Anubhuti: Let us first understand the meaning of the word "bicameralism". It comes from the Latin words "bi" meaning "two" and "camera" meaning "chamber." Therefore, bicameralism, in the context of political institutions, translates to a legislative structure with two houses or chambers. This structure was argued for as a general principle by Montesquieu. His argument was that this dual structure would prevent tyranny of the majority opinion and also ensure a balance of power. He believed that separate legislative bodies could limit the ability of one group to dominate and that they could protect minority interests as well.

This structure, with two chambers, was designed to offer a compromise between the representation of the common people in the lower chamber and the aristocracy and intellectuals in the upper chamber.

He argued that this dual structure allowed for a balance between different interests and groups within society. The lower chamber typically represented the common people, while the upper chamber represented the aristocracy or other specific interests, ensuring a more balanced representation and preventing any single group from dominating the legislature.

He was referring to the British system. Britain adopted bicameralism in multiple steps, starting with the separation of the commons from the nobility and clergy in 1341. This marked the beginning of an Upper and Lower Chamber. The Upper Chamber later became the House of Lords in 1544 and the Lower Chamber became the House of Commons. The House of Lords comprises experts in various fields other than politics, lords and knights nominated by the monarch under the advice of the government, spiritual lords, or clergy, and other public intellectuals and people not affiliated to any political party. All of them are nominated, whereas the House of Commons is equivalent to the Lok Sabha and comprises elected politicians.

Over time, the House of Commons has been slowly gaining more power. In the early 1900s, the power of the House of Lords was reduced from rejecting legislation to merely delaying them. Since then, this has become the standard practice in many nation states. Almost 40% of democratic nation states have a bicameral legislature. Each nation state has its own history, which informs the reason for a bicameral legislature and its nature. Typically, this arrangement is made in geographies that are large and include multiple autonomous states.

Ira: In simple terms what is their contribution to the governance process?

Anubhuti: You see, the logic is that a directly elected government is directed by popular will – like Rousseau calls it, it is the general will of all the citizens of a nation. In principle, the popular government is influenced by this popular will, and is likely to include majoritarian interests which could be partisan – ignoring or downplaying other people’s interests. We need some process to regulate these decisions made by the central government.

India is quite diverse in terms of language, culture, ethnicities, and geographies; and therefore each state’s perceptions of the common laws and policies and their interests will vary. It is possible that some central decisions are contrary to the interests of particular states. We need a structure that ensures these central decisions are vetted and closely scrutinized so that they do not compromise particular contexts.

The Rajya Sabha plays the role of vetting and ensuring that the interests of particular states and union territories are considered in all laws and policies.

Ira: You mean to say that we need the Rajya Sabha, a council of states, to bring in representatives from all the states into the central governance process?

Anubhuti: That is the primary purpose of the Rajya Sabha – to include the diverse complex states into the central decision-making process. In that sense, the Rajya Sabha plays a contributory role in decision-making through oversight, rather than control and monitoring. It would help to refer to the Constituent Assembly debates, when Ananthasayanam Ayyangar was eloquent in his justification for a bicameral legislature.

He said, “Sir, it is common knowledge that in this country so far as we are concerned, there is so much enthusiasm and if for no other reason, we must find opportunity for various people to take part in politics. Therefore, it is necessary that we should have another House where the genius of the people may have full play. The second reason is that whatever hasty legislation is passed by the Lower House may be checkmated by the go-slow movement of the Upper House. The third reason is that the Upper House is a permanent body, while the Lower House is not.”[i]

I find his use of the phrase “checkmated by the go-slow movement” interesting. It mirrors what the House of Lords can do – only delay legislation, not entirely reject it. To my mind, the phrase hasty legislation refers to the argument about the general will. In reality, this seeming adherence to the general will could also be populism – aiming to please the majority. Members of the Lok Sabha are elected keeping national interests in mind; whereas, Members of Legislative Assemblies (MLAs) are elected keeping regional interests (those of the state) in mind. Since the Rajya Sabha members are elected by MLAs, it is essential in a nation as complex as India.

The Rajya Sabha’s assent is required for most legislative decisions, except for some key ones like finance and money bills, where the Lok Sabha has absolute power. All other legislative decisions have to be ratified by the Rajya Sabha too through a majority vote; even the proclamation of national emergency.

Ira: Wow! What foresight! Is there a parallel between the members of the House of Lords being the elite and intellectuals and the intellectuals and artists being nominated to the Rajya Sabha?

Anubhuti: You see, the Rajya Sabha can have a maximum of 250 members and only 12 of them are nominated by the president based on their accomplishments in various areas like arts, culture, social service, economics, literature, etc. For instance, Sudha Murthy, P. T. Usha, and Ilayaraja are currently nominated members of the Rajya Sabha. By the way, Smt. Rukmini Arundale, the founder of Kalakshetra, was the first woman to be nominated to the Rajya Sabha in 1952.

The remaining 238 seats are allotted to the states. To ensure that there is parity between the larger and smaller states, the number of seats is based on population and the number of Lok Sabha constituencies in the state.

One seat is allotted for each million of the first 5 million population. For the population above 5 million, the state gets 1 seat per 2 million. That is why Karnataka has 12 Rajya Sabha seats, Maharashtra has 19, and Uttar Pradesh has 31. Interestingly, these seats are not allotted to any geographical area or constituency.

Ira: Is the Rajya Sabha election also political in nature? Not just central politics, but local politics also with people from different parties representing different groups and different interests?

Anubhuti: Of course, being a multi-party democracy, both houses have members from different political parties. The idea is that the Rajya Sabha members from each state are from different political parties, and each party gets a representation that is proportional to the number of seats they have in the State Legislative Assembly. You see, the idea is that the composition of the Rajya Sabha in a way subverts the composition of the Lok Sabha. Though the members come from the same party, the interests are different – one being particular to the state they come from and the other vouching for national interests.

Ira: So, the political parties nominate members from across the state for these RS seats and the MLAs vote for them? How does this ensure proportional representation? It is possible some nominees of a party do not win the RS election at all.

Anubhuti: Actually, the election process is different here; it is not the usual majority votes or first past the post. The RS elections are done using the mechanism of single transferable vote. On the ballot paper, the voting MLA has to indicate an order of preference of each person standing for election in each seat. It is expected that a party MLA puts the nominee from his party as first preference, but they are allowed to mark nominees of other parties also.

To win the election, the votes where the candidate has first preference are counted. If they meet the threshold required and have more votes than other nominees, they win the election. The threshold is determined by an interesting formula in order to maintain representation of each political party in proportion to other parties.

Threshold #votes = (Total votes polled / (Total number of candidates to be elected + 1)) + 1

If they do not meet the threshold or do not get a majority, the nominee who has least preference is cancelled, and those votes are distributed among the other candidates. A lot of mathematics is done to ensure proportional representation.

Ira: When you say proportional, you mean there will be a correlation between the percentage of seats each party has in the Legislative Assembly, and the percentage in the Rajya Sabha. So that way the RS truly brings representation from each state in proportion to representation of all political parties from each state.

Anubhuti: Yes. The point of this process is that the Rajya Sabha has to represent all states, so that they can review legislations using their region’s language, culture, etc., and ensure that the legislation does not violate or downplay their diverse needs and interests.

Ira: You are right; maintaining the federal nature of the Union of India is not an easy task. A complex process like this is needed for a complex nation like ours to continue being a democracy. Our constitution challenges and expands the very notion of democracy.

[i]https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/03-jan-1949/#94934

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
Ira and Anubhuti discuss how India’s Upper House is not merely symbolic, but essential to fair representation in our complex, plural democracy.

item.title: Every student counts: unlocking the power of inclusive classrooms
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

In today’s dynamic and diverse educational landscape, the concept of an inclusive classroom is more relevant than ever. Classrooms now mirror the rich variety in society, bringing together students of different abilities, backgrounds, languages, and learning styles. For teachers, this presents both a challenge and an opportunity: to ensure every student feels valued, supported, and empowered to achieve their best. The true spirit of inclusion goes far beyond simply having all students present in the same space. It is about creating an environment where everyone participates meaningfully and experiences a genuine sense of belonging.

Research has shown that inclusive classrooms benefit all students. When teachers intentionally foster inclusion, academic outcomes improve, and students develop greater empathy and a stronger sense of community. Inclusion is not just about supporting students with disabilities or those from diverse backgrounds; it is about ensuring that every learner – regardless of their starting point – has the chance to thrive. This approach prepares students for life in a global society, where collaboration, respect for differences, and adaptability are essential skills.

At the heart of an inclusive classroom is the teacher’s commitment to understand each learner. This means taking the time to get to know students’ strengths, challenges, and unique perspectives. Teachers who are attuned to the individual needs of their students – whether related to learning preferences, language barriers, or cultural backgrounds – can make informed decisions about how best to support them. For example, a student who is shy about speaking in front of the class might participate more confidently in small group discussions, while another who struggles with written instructions may benefit from visual or verbal cues. These small, thoughtful adjustments can make a world of difference.

A supportive classroom environment is built on respect, safety, and inclusion. When students feel emotionally secure, they are more willing to take risks, ask questions, and engage deeply with learning. Teachers can nurture this environment by establishing clear expectations, valuing every student’s contribution, and using community-building activities that foster connection and trust. Celebrating diversity – whether through cultural events, sharing stories, or simply acknowledging different perspectives – helps every student feel seen and appreciated.

Routine and structure are also essential components of an inclusive classroom. Many students, especially those who struggle with transitions or uncertainty, benefit from knowing what to expect each day. Posting daily schedules, giving advance notice before changes, and using visual aids or symbols to clarify routines can help reduce anxiety and create a sense of stability for all learners.

Instructional flexibility is key to reaching every student. Two powerful approaches are Universal Design for Learning (UDL) and differentiated instruction. UDL is a proactive strategy that anticipates diverse learning needs from the outset, offering content in multiple formats – such as visual, auditory, and hands-on activities – so that all students can access the material. Differentiated instruction, on the other hand, is a responsive approach that tailors teaching in real time based on ongoing assessment of students’ needs. By combining these strategies, teachers can ensure that lessons are accessible, engaging, and challenging for every learner.

Technology has become an invaluable tool for inclusion. Digital resources, interactive displays, and accessible content can make learning more engaging and adaptable to individual needs. For example, captioned videos support students with hearing impairments or those learning a new language, while alternative input methods for written work help students with physical or learning challenges participate fully. Technology also opens up creative avenues for students to demonstrate their understanding, whether through multimedia presentations, digital storytelling, or collaborative online projects.

Collaborative learning further enhances inclusion by encouraging teamwork and peer support. When students work together in thoughtfully structured groups, they learn to appreciate each other’s strengths and perspectives. Strategies like “think-pair-share” allow even the quietest students to contribute, while group projects foster a sense of shared responsibility and achievement. Teachers can maximize the benefits of collaboration by assigning roles that play to students’ individual abilities and by creating opportunities for everyone to lead and support their peers.

Culturally responsive teaching is another vital aspect of inclusion. By integrating students’ backgrounds and experiences into lessons, teachers validate their identities and enrich the learning environment. Using examples from various cultures, inviting students to share their stories, and connecting learning to real-world contexts all help build a classroom culture of respect and appreciation for diversity.

Assessment practices should also reflect the principles of inclusion. Rather than relying solely on traditional tests or written assignments, teachers can offer multiple ways for students to demonstrate their understanding. This might include oral presentations, practical demonstrations, creative projects, or visual representations. By providing choices, teachers ensure that assessments measure true learning and not just proficiency in one mode of expression.

Inclusiveness is not a one-time achievement but an ongoing journey. Teachers should regularly reflect on their practices, seek feedback from students and colleagues, and be willing to adapt as needs evolve. Continuous professional development, collaboration with other educators, and openness to new ideas are all part of building a more inclusive classroom.

The contemporary relevance of inclusiveness in education cannot be overstated. As classrooms become more diverse, the need for intentional, equitable teaching grows. Inclusive classrooms help students develop the resilience, confidence, and sense of belonging they need to succeed – not just academically, but as compassionate and capable members of society. The COVID-19 pandemic, for instance, highlighted the importance of emotional wellbeing and community in learning. Now, more than ever, students need classrooms that are safe, supportive, and responsive to their individual needs.

Ultimately, inclusive classrooms do not happen by accident. They are built intentionally, day by day, through small changes, empathy, and a steadfast commitment to equity. Every adjustment, every act of understanding, and every celebration of diversity brings us closer to classrooms where all students can learn, grow, and contribute. Teachers have the power to be agents of inclusion, transforming lives and shaping a brighter, more connected future for everyone.

Let us work together to make inclusion a lived reality in every classroom – today and for the future. By unlocking the power of inclusive classrooms, we ensure that every student truly counts.

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A GenZ in spirit, with the benefit of hindsight, she uses her 37 years in media and education to help leverage generational diversity at work and at home. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
What makes a classroom inclusive? Neerja Singh lists small changes that can transform classrooms to empathetic spaces where every student participates and feels safe.
