item.title: Where is home?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
The role of global education in a borderless world

Monica Kochar

The world is more connected than ever. Technology, travel, and communication have made it possible for people to interact across borders. In classrooms, students come from different cultural backgrounds, speak different languages, and bring unique perspectives. Teachers need to prepare students for this global reality.

A global mindset helps teachers understand the world beyond their own experiences. It enables them to teach with cultural awareness, encourage open-mindedness, and help students think beyond their immediate surroundings. Students who develop a global perspective will be better prepared for future careers, international collaborations, and cross-cultural understanding.

Having a global mindset benefits both teachers and students by:

  1. Encouraging respect for different cultures.
  2. Promoting critical thinking about global issues.
  3. Building empathy and inclusivity in the classroom.
  4. Adapting teaching strategies for diverse learners.
  5. Helping students see connections between their lives and the wider world.
  6. Preparing students for careers in a global economy.
  7. Identifying and addressing biases in teaching materials.

How can teachers develop a global mindset?

  1. Learn about other cultures
    1. Read about different countries and traditions.
    1. Talk to people from diverse backgrounds.
    1. Travel virtually through books, documentaries, and online resources.
    1. Ask: How can I bring cultural awareness into my classroom?
  2. Encourage open discussions
    1. Discuss world events with students.
    1. Ask students about their own cultural experiences.
    1. Create a safe space where students feel comfortable sharing perspectives.
    1. Ask: How can I ensure respectful and inclusive classroom discussions?
  3. Use global examples in lessons
    1. Include stories, case studies, and examples from different parts of the world.
    1. Show students how global issues affect them.
    1. Integrate international perspectives in assignments and discussions.
    1. Ask: How does my teaching reflect global diversity?
  4. Learn a new language
    1. Even basic knowledge of another language helps in understanding different perspectives.
    1. Encourage students to appreciate multiple languages.
    1. Support multilingual learners in their language development.
    1. Ask: How can I integrate language learning into my teaching?
  5. Connect with educators worldwide
    1. Join international teacher networks.
    1. Participate in online collaborations.
    1. Engage in professional development courses focused on global education.
    1. Ask: How can I learn from teachers in other countries?
  6. Encourage students to think globally
    1. Include discussions on topics like climate change, migration, and human rights.
    1. Use technology to connect with classrooms in other countries.
    1. Help students see the impact of global issues in their lives.
    1. Ask: How can I prepare students for a globally connected future?

Global mindset for math teachers

Mathematics is a universal language. It connects people across cultures, industries, and fields of study. Math teachers have a unique opportunity to help students see the subject through a global lens.

1. Highlight the global history of mathematics

  • Teach students about mathematical contributions from different cultures (e.g., Indian numerals, Arabic algebra, Chinese geometry, Mayan calendars).
  • Show how mathematical ideas travelled across civilizations and influenced each other.
  • Ask: How can I make the history of math more inclusive and diverse?

2. Use real-world global problems

  • Incorporate math problems related to international issues such as climate change, economics, and population growth.
  • Show how math is used to solve global challenges like disease modelling or renewable energy development.
  • Ask: How can I use math to make students aware of global challenges?

3. Introduce culturally diverse problem-solving methods

  • Explore different mathematical techniques used across cultures (e.g., Vedic math from India, the Russian Peasant method of multiplication).
  • Encourage students to compare and analyze various problem-solving strategies.
  • Ask: How can I introduce multiple mathematical perspectives in my lessons?

4. Use data from around the world

  • Include graphs, statistics, and case studies from different countries.
  • Have students analyze economic trends, population growth, or environmental changes in various regions.
  • Encourage critical thinking about how numbers reflect social and political realities.
  • Ask: How can I make math more relevant through global data?

5. Collaborate with international classrooms

  • Partner with schools in different countries for joint math projects.
  • Use online tools to share mathematical discoveries and problem-solving approaches.
  • Compare how math is taught in different education systems.
  • Ask: How can I use global connections to enhance math learning?

The future of education: teaching for a globalized world

Education is no longer limited to one country or culture. Teachers must prepare students for a world where working across borders is common. By promoting a global mindset, teachers can help students see the world as a shared space of learning, growth, and connection.

Reflection

  • What steps can I take today to build a global mindset?
  • How can I help my students develop cultural awareness?
  • How does my teaching support students in becoming global citizens?
  • How can I incorporate global perspectives into my subject area?
  • What small change can I make this week to make my classroom more inclusive?

A global mindset does not happen overnight. But small, consistent steps can make a big difference. Teachers who embrace this shift will not only improve their own teaching but will also help students thrive in an interconnected world.

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
What does it mean to belong in a global classroom? Monica Kochar writes about how teachers can help students feel at home anywhere.

item.title: From chalk to chatbot: a teacher's guide to NotebookLM
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anuradha C

In the ever-evolving landscape of education, teachers are constantly expected to do more – in less time. From lesson planning and grading to staying up-to-date with new policies and pedagogical techniques, the burden is real. For Indian school teachers, especially those navigating large classrooms, tight schedules, and limited resources, the pressure can be even more intense.

Enter NotebookLM – a free AI-powered note-taking and learning tool from Google. While it may sound like "just another app," it’s much more than that. NotebookLM can become your personal teaching assistant, research aide, and lesson planning partner – all in one neat platform.

But don’t just take our word for it. Let’s meet Mrs. Latha Krishna, a seasoned high school science teacher from Bengaluru, who earlier dismissed NotebookLM as a tech gimmick, but now swears by it.

A teacher's reluctant start with NotebookLM

Mrs. Latha Krishna had been teaching for over 20 years. Her students loved her, but she often stayed back after school hours to plan lessons, look up the latest NEP guidelines, and prepare her own training materials for in-service teacher development programs.

When a younger colleague suggested she try NotebookLM to save time, her immediate reaction was skepticism. “AI? I’m not a techie,” she chuckled. “I’ve been managing just fine with my handwritten notes and Google searches.”

But one late evening, with a chapter on genetics due for revision and her eyes tired from cross-referencing five textbooks, she decided to give it a shot.

She uploaded her PDFs, notes, and previous year's question papers into NotebookLM. What happened next was nothing short of transformative.

What is NotebookLM?

NotebookLM (short for “Language Model”) is a smart notebook powered by Google AI. It allows you to upload your documents – be it lesson plans, PDFs, curriculum guidelines, or scanned notes – and then ask questions based on them. The AI doesn’t give generic answers from the internet; it answers based on your uploaded materials.

So if you upload your class 10 CBSE biology notes, you can ask:

  • “Summarize this chapter in bullet points.”
  • “Generate quiz questions from this lesson.”
  • “What are the key differences between mitosis and meiosis from my notes?”

You can even ask it to:

  • Help draft lesson plans.
  • Create analogies for difficult concepts.
  • Translate content into regional languages.
  • Suggest activity-based learning ideas.

How teachers can use NotebookLM in daily work

Smarter lesson planning

Instead of sifting through stacks of books and webpages, upload your preferred content and ask NotebookLM to generate lesson summaries, highlight key points, and suggest examples or classroom activities. It saves hours and improves clarity.

Create teaching aids in minutes

From quizzes and crossword puzzles to concept maps and classroom discussion prompts – NotebookLM can help you generate these quickly, customized to your syllabus.

Your partner in continuing education

Teachers are lifelong learners. Whether it’s reading new education research, preparing for a professional development workshop, or updating your knowledge on the NEP, you can upload papers and policies and ask NotebookLM to explain, summarize, or test your understanding.

Simplify complex topics for students

Want to explain Einstein’s theory of relativity to 9th graders? Or break down Newton’s Laws with local examples? Just ask NotebookLM to do it in simple language or regional metaphors.

Latha Krishna’s NotebookLM journey: from fear to fascination

After a week’s use, Mrs. Krishna’s verdict changed completely.

“I used to take three hours to prep a class. Now I do it in 45 minutes – with more depth and creativity,” she said. “I even used it to prepare for a teacher training session on integrating AI into classrooms!”

She now conducts AI awareness sessions in her school, helping fellow teachers across subjects – math, English, history, even physical education – understand how NotebookLM can complement their teaching methods.

Getting started is easy

  1. Go to https://notebooklm.google.
  2. Sign in with your Google account.
  3. Upload your teaching materials (PDFs, notes, slides, etc.).
  4. Start asking questions or requesting summaries, quizzes, translations, etc.

Pro tip: Create different notebooks for each class or subject for easier organization.

Addressing common concerns

“Will AI replace me?”

No. Think of it as your assistant, not your competitor. AI can’t replace the emotional intelligence, cultural context, and mentorship you provide to students.

“Is it safe and private?”

NotebookLM is designed to be private and works only with the content you upload. It doesn't pull in random web content unless you ask it to.

“I don’t have high-speed internet or fancy devices.”

NotebookLM works on basic laptops and even mid-range Android phones. It’s light and efficient.

Final thoughts

Just like how calculators didn't make math teachers obsolete but helped them teach better, NotebookLM won't replace teachers – it will empower them.

Mrs. Latha Krishna is now one of the most progressive educators in her school – not because she knows the most tech, but because she chose to evolve.

So if you're a teacher reading this, give NotebookLM a try. You don’t have to be a tech wizard to get started. Start small, explore, and let it grow with you.

Because the best teachers never stop learning.

Have questions or want help getting started with NotebookLM for your class? Write to us or contact your school’s IT facilitator.

Anuradha C is an IT industry drop-out after several years of slogging and money-making. She is now working freelance as a corporate technical trainer and content writer. She is hoping to channelize her passion for writing into a satisfying experience for herself and a joyous experience for her readers. She can be reached at anuradhac@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anuradha C
Here’s how one teacher went from fearing AI to leading staff training. She simplified her curriculum planning while saving time.

item.title: From cover to content: how to choose the right ELT coursebook for your students
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sanjhee Gianchandani

Good textbooks provide learners with authentic experiences that are critical to their learning hence the selection of the correct teaching materials is a crucial part of the teaching-learning continuum. Krishna Kumar, former Director of the National Council for Educational Research and Training (NCERT), opines, “The first aspect is the symbolic value of the textbook – as a document authorised by the state. The policy of prescribing a single textbook for each subject started in the mid-19th century. When the present-day system of education was taking shape, the colonial state found in the printed textbook a reliable means of standardising pedagogy, learning, and evaluation. This is how the system has functioned for a long time, and the idea that the textbook is the curriculum has stayed firmly lodged in the teacher’s mind.”

In most Indian schools, regardless of the board being followed, there are three books prescribed for English – a main coursebook, a grammar book, and a workbook. There may sometimes be a supplementary reader as well. There is also a teacher’s manual exclusively for teachers, which has the answer keys, lesson plans, and guidelines for using the main course and grammar books. Nowadays, most publishers also include digital content in the form of extra reading material or app-based activities as well.

Most English educators today face the important responsibility of reviewing materials, particularly textbooks, to determine the best options for their classrooms. To make informed decisions, they must understand the intricacies involved in creating an effective English main coursebook. In this article, we will analyze the structure of a main coursebook in detail, highlighting the essential components that contribute to its effectiveness in fostering language learning.

Core elements of an ELT main coursebook

A typical main coursebook in English has the following structure – a table of contents where chapter titles and page numbers are listed, followed by a detailed table of contents where the subskills of each section, the specific tasks for LSRW (Listening, Speaker, Reading, Writing), and the learning outcomes are listed, and finally, the chapters. The learning is usually staged with each task based on a particular learning outcome and a particular skill in focus. There are generally 12 chapters in an ELT main coursebook comprising 4 poems and 8 stories. Educators need to get a sense of the learning outcomes for each section in each chapter, and they can do this by reading the text and the outcomes in conjunction and referring to them repeatedly to gain a more nuanced understanding of the content.

Instructional design for ELT coursebooks

A typical chapter in an ELT coursebook has all of the following sections in the same or similar chronological order but with different names:

  1. Warm up – This section includes a small fun activity that is thematically linked to the chapter. The purpose of this activity is to activate the schema and to orient the students’ thinking in the direction of the chapter.
  2. Listening – All ELT books will have listening exercises as they contribute to the overall linguistic proficiency. Most books include age-appropriate dialogues (conversations, radio interviews, discussions, debates, etc.) or monologues (airport/railway station announcements, podcasts, presentations, etc.) for students to listen to and solve a ‘while-listening exercise’ such as an MCQ (Multiple-choice question), or a gap-fill. The listening scripts could be located either at the back of the book or in the teacher’s manual.
  3. Reading – This section consists of the story or poem. It has all difficult vocabulary items highlighted and glossed as footnotes or after the text for reference. There may also be some short in-text questions to test comprehension while reading. Sometimes, reading texts are followed by a short “About the Author” section, which supplies information about the author.
  4. Vocabulary – Students engage with glossed words as well as sight words by attempting short exercises in this section.
  5. Comprehension – This includes objective as well as subjective questions linked to the text.
  6. Pronunciation – This section picks up sounds that the students have heard in the story, makes them practise those sounds in isolation, and then gives exercises on the target sound.
  7. Grammar – A good ELT book highlights the target language in the chapter and begins this section by emphasizing it. It follows the EEE approach – explanations, followed by examples, and then guided and unguided exercises for students to attempt to cement their understanding of the concepts.
  8. Writing – This section encompasses one of the writing skills that is appropriate for the chapter and is included in the syllabus. First, there is an explanation of the skill, then there is usually a sample or a template, and this is followed by practice exercises. There will also be scaffolding in the form of clues, prompts, and sentence starters with some questions meant to support students in their writing.
  9. Speaking – This section makes students talk among themselves (to the whole class or in groups or pairs) using guidelines, sentence starters, and templates. Sample texts are also provided sometimes, or there is a cross-reference to the listening section from where the students can pick the target language.
  10. Art integration / project work – These sections are a recent addition following the guidelines of the NEP (National Education Policy). Here, students could be made to draw, make a piece of art, or do something outside of school.

These sections, when viewed in totality, ensure that the learning outcomes are met and help assess a student’s grasp of the language. The concomitant workbook has exercises based on each chapter. This may be in the form of worksheets or tests. The grammar book explains each grammar topic touched upon in this book in further detail.

The review workflow

Let us assume that an educator needs to review an ELT main coursebook. It may seem like a daunting task, but if broken down into logical steps, it could be made both easy and interesting. Here is a sequence that could be followed:

  1. Give the chapter a free read. At this stage, the general language level should be carefully checked ensuring that the vocabulary/structures align with what is prearranged for the target grade.
  2. Ask some initial questions: “What should the target learners be able to do in and with the target language?” or “What knowledge about language and what guidance for using language appropriately for different purposes in various situations are offered in the materials?”
  3. Check the rubrics carefully and decide if learners have all the information required to proceed with a particular task. If anything is amiss, determine the kind of support you would need to provide them as an educator.
  4. Set your expectations about whether the materials enable or limit your freedom as an educator and the strategies you want to use to teach the language.
  5. Also, consider if the selection of texts meets the learners' interests. For example, a text about Scottish kilns may not work in a school in a tier-2 city in India. Texts must provide all learners with a fair chance to develop their language, knowledge, and capabilities.

Quick reference guide

Here is a ready reckoner to check the material while reviewing. Educators must ask themselves the following questions while reviewing:

In conclusion, gaining a deeper understanding of the intricacies of ELT coursebooks is vital for educators who are responsible for selecting the most effective materials for their classrooms. By exploring the structure of an ELT book, educators can appreciate how each component contributes to language learning. The review process is crucial for identifying resources that align with pedagogical goals, ensuring that the chosen materials meet the diverse needs of students. The ready reckoner is a practical tool for educators, providing key criteria to streamline their evaluation process. Finally, staying informed about emerging trends in language teaching can guide educators in selecting innovative and relevant coursebooks. Ultimately, by considering these factors, educators can make informed choices that significantly enhance the learning experience and empower students to master the English language.

References

  • https://eltnews.gr/post/5-1-valuable-tips-to-consider-before-choosing-a-course-book/
  • https://www.thehinducentre.com/the-arena/textbooks-and-the-curriculum-understanding-the-politics/article67039035.ece

Sanjhee Gianchandani works as an English language curriculum designer and editor. She holds a Master’s degree in English from Lady Shri Ram College for Women, University of Delhi and a CELTA from the University of Cambridge. Her rich experience ranges from working as an English language assessment specialist to being a Writing/Speaking examiner for various international examinations to leading examiner/marker cohorts and finally managing content for the school segment. She has authored three series: Grammar Sparkle, Let’s Listen and Speak, and Epic English Grammar. Additionally, she is empanelled as a consultant editor with various publishing houses across the country. She has edited over 100 books ranging from academic to fiction, non-fiction, poetry, and children’s writing. She is passionate about writing and her articles on ELT pedagogy and learning strategies have been published in several educational magazines, journals, and blogs. She can be reached at sanjheegianchandani28@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sanjhee Gianchandani
What should teachers look for in a coursebook? Sanjhee Gianchandani shares different components and assessment tools for language learning.

item.title: Divaswapna: A must-read that instructs and delights
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Simran Luthra

Gijubhai Badheka’s Divaswapna: An Experiment in Education has delighted readers over several decades. Recently, Mamata Pandya, Gijubhai’s granddaughter, has translated the book afresh from its original Gujarati into English. This edition, published by Banyan Tree, carries a foreword by Arvind Gupta and also includes a detailed biographical note on Gijubhai’s extraordinary life and work which helps to contextualize this work further and enhances one’s appreciation of his ideas. The original foreword by the author from the first Gujarati edition published in 1931 clarifies that the book was the result of Gijubhai’s attempt to write about his thoughts on education in the form of a story instead of serious academic essays.

The delightful classic has the following key characters: Laxmiram (our hero), the writer turned experimental educator, the progressive and idealistic Education Officer, the conservative and perpetually anxious Head Master, the mocking and envious fellow teachers, and the students of class 4 who are the subjects of Laxmiram’s experiment.

In this article, I share some reflections from my reading of the book and offer insights into how Gijubhai’s ideas resonate with contemporary educational challenges, making it more relevant than ever for teachers and educators.

The continued relevance of the book

While it is great for the book, it is unfortunate that it is still so highly relatable. What this means is that things have not changed and the same preoccupations that troubled Gijubhai continue to be what we struggle with today as well.

For instance, early on in the book, Gijubhai describes the intense dislike children have for school. He tells the Headmaster when the latter begins questioning his methods at the start of the year: “They [students] do not like school at all. It took just one word ‘holiday’ for them to exalt and escape.” However, just like the bleak reality that persists from Gijubhai’s time to our own, the insights Gijubhai shares through Laxmiram also continue to be immensely relevant and meaningful.

Experiments with pedagogy

“Those who can, do; those who can’t, teach.” Perhaps meant humorously, these disparaging words about the teaching profession from Bernard Shaw’s play, Man and Superman, have been taken a bit too seriously over the years. So much so that there is a popular perception that anyone can become a teacher. It is, therefore, heartening that on the very first page of Divaswapna we find the Education Officer telling Laxmiram, who is a writer but an educator at heart, “It is easy to sit at a table and write articles. It is also easy to imagine that you are a teacher. What is difficult is to actually do this, and do it successfully.” Perhaps, the biggest achievement of Divaswapna is that it brings out the complexity of the work that is teaching. And further, it also tells the reader that teaching ought to be carried out thoughtfully if it is to go beyond rote learning and becoming oppressive.

One of the most fascinating and encouraging aspects of Divaswapna is Laxmiram’s experimentation with pedagogy and the successes that he encounters. Laxmiram does what so many teachers dream of… he rejects the textbook-centric approach to teaching, engages with disciplines and concepts, and thinks of suitable pedagogic approaches to teach them so that the result is not just rote memorization.

Gijubhai, in fact, touches upon some deep aspects of learning. Through Laxmiram he shows us that learning is natural for human beings and how the artificial approaches of schooling can systematically damage and even destroy this natural urge and instinct.

Understanding ‘classroom management’

A common struggle for teachers is managing classrooms full of students who are unable to regulate themselves simply because they are children. Classroom norms such as expecting ‘pin drop’ silence, minimal bodily movements, and consistent attention from students are in fact extremely restrictive and do not align with how children naturally engage and learn.

More often than not, teachers ensure such behaviour by exerting their authority and power over students in the classroom. Laxmiram intuitively knows that this is not his path. He understands that teaching involves human-to-human connection. He says to himself: “First, I need to get acquainted with my students. I need to awaken their interest, and some affection towards me. Only then might they listen to me and do what I tell them.”

Laxmiram begins by telling stories to his class both to engage them and to endear himself. Soon enough, the Education Officer hears of this and asks him when he is going to actually start teaching beyond telling stories, and Laxmiram responds by saying that this is in fact part of teaching; that he is ensuring orderly behaviour among students by engaging them through stories so that they are engaged and then become ready to learn.

Teaching through stories

Stories, in fact, are a key pedagogic tool that Laxmiram wields. He leverages the power of stories first to manage the classroom and later to teach history. All along, the Education Officer and Headmaster display their extreme discomfort with stories as they perceive them almost as a base form of entertainment.

However, stories are built into the human DNA. Since ancient times, stories have been used to make sense of the world and transmit knowledge across generations. It comes as no surprise, then, that the students in Laxmiram’s class sit in rapt attention when he tells them stories. So, when one of his initial classes goes well and he is free of the ruckus the students were creating otherwise, he thinks to himself: “Well today went well. A story is truly magic. Nothing can be truer than this.”

The power of games and intrinsic motivation

Similar to the power of stories, Laxmiram understands that games too, hold immense pedagogic value. Not to be confused with the superficial emphasis placed nowadays on ‘gamification’ of education (which can also be a bit manipulative), Laxmiram is interested in the character-building potential of games that is associated with sports.

Once again as with his use of stories, games are also met with resistance from the Headmaster. Trying to convince the Headmaster, Laxmiram makes an impassioned argument: “Sir, games are a form of true education. Many great forces in the world have developed on the playing field. Games build character.”

Importantly, Laxmiram is clear that the games he makes his students play are not centred around competition. He clarifies to the students: “No talk about winning and losing. It is a game. Sometimes we are weaker, sometimes others are. There are no winners and losers. Games are for playing, for running around and having fun. There is no place for winning and losing, and fighting.”

Performativity in schools

Related to the themes of non-competition and intrinsic motivation is also Laxmiram’s position on the practice within schools of putting up a show (literally) for authorities to demonstrate the progress and competence of students. Thus, students are handpicked for the Commissioner’s visit where they would put up performances with dialogues, recite poems, perform drills, and so on. This is to be followed by giving prizes to the best and rewards to the rest.

Laxmiram provides scathing commentary and uses terms such as ‘deception’ and ‘farce’ to describe efforts to showcase learning and competence that are actually the result of rehearsal and rote. He even goes to the extent of forbidding any students from his class to participate. The Headmaster justifies it saying it is ‘tradition’. This is a good point for the reader to pause and reflect on several ‘traditions’ that are part of the school routine that are inauthentic or meaningless.

The experimental teacher is not against showcasing learning. A little later in the year when the Education Officer is coming as an examiner during the half-yearly exams, Laxmiram is eager for his students to display all that they have learned. However, it is not a performance. He tells them: “You have to do today also, exactly what we do every day. You have all already passed. Today I have just invited everyone to see our work.” This is the sort of authentic, non-competitive ethic of learning Laxmiram believes in.

This is Laxmiram’s approach to learning. He is against the idea of external validation of learning. Hence, when his class performs a play of high calibre before the Education Officer, the latter says: “I won’t give them prizes. The genuine pleasure they felt while acting, is a greater and better reward than anything else.” This is precisely what Laxmiram aims to cultivate in his students: a joy of learning driven by intrinsic motivation rather than external rewards.

Dealing with the system

Besides being an idealist, Laxmiram is also a shrewd realist. At various points we see him having to deal with a system he does not agree with. He encounters seniors, authority figures, fellow teachers, as well as parents. He deals with them with tact for the most part, although there are moments when he has impassioned outbursts.

Encouragement for teachers

One significant moment is when after the half-year exams, though impressed by his efforts, the fellow teachers express their lack of will in being as innovative and creative as Laxmiram. He tells them that even more than what he has accomplished is possible in primary education. He tells them something profound: “The key is common sense and innovation, and that comes from the yearning in our soul for something.”

Once, when they come back from playing a game, he is told by a fellow teacher: It is best to keep these children within four walls, and make them study and learn by rote. Sadly, this perspective still persists largely among schools and parents. Gijubhai, however, exercises a most pragmatic view of learning and education and shows how through creative engagement and efforts, school education can become enjoyable and meaningful.

He insists that if teachers teach well in school, there would be no need for tuition classes after school. The teachers tell him that they need tuitions to supplement their meagre salaries [the proverbial teacher’s salary]. In response, Laxmiram urges the teachers to be bold and demand a higher salary. He nearly inspires them to start a public movement to improve the status of teachers.

Laxmiram also proposes that exams should be conducted by teachers as it is they who know best the strengths and weaknesses of their students, hinting at one aspect of teacher autonomy, which has progressively reduced and is at an all-time low at present.

Critiquing the curriculum and existing methods

Time and again Laxmiram is seen to be exerting what has been a precious commodity for teachers: autonomy. Most often, teachers accept the curriculum and system as a given and rarely question things. Our hero, however, looks upon everything with a critical eye because he has a clear philosophy of education.

Even today, teachers have very little institutional space to critique and appraise textbooks. Laxmiram looks at history textbooks and points out factual errors in one and criticizes the ‘old-fashioned approach’ in another. Since he is conducting an experiment, he has the space to replace textbooks with storytelling to make history enjoyable for his students.

Krishna Kumar has used the term ‘textbook regime’ to indicate the supremacy and stronghold that textbooks have in Indian classrooms. We would do well to take a leaf from Gijubhai’s Divaswapna and treat textbooks not as the word of God, but as fallible texts written by people who have their own biases and limitations.

Questioning the syllabus, Laxmiram asks why class four students would be interested in sentence parsing and questions the relevance of teaching formal grammar to them. Understanding this basic principle of relevance and interest, Laxmiram proceeds to teach formal grammar, albeit through games and activities so that at least his students are able to do well in the exams.

Later in the book, Gijubhai also discusses the value of learning by observation. This is a form of learning that is severely neglected today. However, this is an important way in which human beings naturally learn in real-world environments. Historically, apprenticeships used this method to learn skills by placing novices alongside experienced practitioners. Experimenting with and being open to different pedagogic styles is certainly something Laxmiram inspires teachers to do.

Views on examinations

We also frequently hear Gijubhai fret over exams. He knows that his experiments in teaching-learning would be judged by how his students will fare in the examinations, a reality that still exists.

It is vital to understand that ultimately assessments shape the way teachers teach. Thus, while there are discourses of ‘learning with fun’ or ‘joyful learning’ or ‘learning without burden’, they have not been able to make a deep enough impact because the linked assessment system has not fundamentally changed. Ultimately, if the success of both students and teachers is going to be judged on examination results, pedagogy will be shaped by what is tested and how it is tested.

Thus, while Laxmiram tailors his pedagogy based on his teaching ideology, he always keeps the exams as the end goal. It is only towards the end of the book, in his conversation with other teachers that he makes important suggestions such as assessments being an important tool for evaluating gaps and other ideas that are still radical such as open book tests, making tests voluntary, and eliminating ranks.

Laxmiram, like the rest of us, knows that children will remember what they have learnt till the examination day after which they will forget the lessons. This is something that bothers him and he decides to work with the aim of ensuring that his students learn experientially and through stories so as to never forget the concepts. The permanence or even longevity of what students learn in school is an aspect that does not bother most people. We often hear the lament that people do not remember what they have studied at school. However, little has been done to address this systemic flaw.

Dealing with parents

At one point, Laxmiram meets 40 parents to convince them of his innovative methods. He speaks to them regarding ‘real learning’ and ‘wrong education’, the relation between academic progress and hygiene, games and character building, and the importance of discipline. He critiques the existing education system that has failed to have a real-world perspective. The Education Officer witnessing Laxmiram’s impassioned interaction with the parents humorously remarks that talking to them about all this was like reading the Bhagavad Gita to a buffalo.

Parents, who are in fact key stakeholders in the education of their children, tend to be the most conservative and rigid when it comes to innovation in education. This holds true even today. A good example of this is parents enthusiastically choosing international curricula such as the International Baccalaureate (IB), but later displaying discomfort with it as they do not have examinations, homework, or textbooks in the manner of Indian school boards. Clearly, getting parents on board with innovative curricula and methods remains a challenge from Gijubhai’s times. But Laxmiram indicates how persistent dialogue is the only way forward with parents.

Beyond the last page…

Daisaku Ikeda, the former head of a Buddhist organization believed that religion exists for the sake of humanity and not the other way round. One can similarly replace education with religion: schools exist for the sake of children and not the other way round. At one point Laxmiram discussing certain children from his class says: “It is not that they are unfit for schools, rather this school is not fit for them. The school does not give them the kind of work that they are capable of.” His stance against corporal punishment also rests on a similar belief. He asks whether it is possible that, if we teach properly, corporal punishment may not be necessary at all.

Gijubhai’s educational philosophy rests on deep trust in and respect for children. A humanist’s conviction shines through on each page of Divaswapna. The book is a source of inspiration and encouragement for anyone who wants to shake school education out of its inertia and make it something that adds true value.

Teachers are largely conformists. They mostly work from the premise of upholding the system, ensuring its seamless execution and functioning. However, unlike a factory process, education is a social good. It is incumbent upon teachers to engage with the processes of schooling critically. Gijubhai’s Laxmiram sees through the system of education, calls out its flaws, and addresses them through his robust philosophy of education and learning.

We are witnessing a large-scale deskilling of teachers today. Teachers are expected to ‘transact’ the curriculum through textbooks or digital content. There is hardly any expectation of teachers to be ‘public intellectuals’, to borrow Giroux’s term. For a teacher to question is a radical act. However, if and when teachers do question, they need support from the school management and parents. School leaders and managements must enable autonomy and creative freedom to teachers. It is high time that Gijubhai’s Divaswapna or daytime reverie becomes a reality.

The book is available at www.banyantreebookstore.com.

Simran Luthra holds a PhD in Education from the School of Education, Tata Institute of Social Sciences (TISS), Mumbai. She also has a Master’s in English from Jadavpur University, Kolkata, and a Master’s in Education from TISS, Mumbai. A former Fulbright Foreign Language Teaching Assistant at Stanford University (2013-14), Simran has over a decade of experience in the field of education. Currently based in Pune, she continues to contribute to education through her research, teaching, and writing. She can be reached at simranluthra@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Simran Luthra
Gijubhai Badheka’s Divaswapna reimagines classrooms through stories and games. Simran Luthra writes about how this classic is more relevant than ever, asking us to challenge the norms of schooling.

item.title: Inject critical thinking into the classroom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aruna Sankaranarayanan

So far, in this column, I have covered curiosity, creativity, compassion, composure, communication, ecological consciousness, and responsible citizenship. That’s a total of seven skills or competencies that are not given adequate emphasis by most mainstream schools. Though this column is called The 7 Cs, I would like to add three more facets and I’m grateful that Teacher Plus has generously granted me the space. The column will continue to be called The 7 Cs as I discuss critical thinking, cultural sensitivity, and collaboration.

Importance of critical thinking

In today’s world, where we have a plethora of information at our fingertips, along with misinformation and disinformation, critical thinking is indeed an essential skill. In Critical Thinking, Tom Chatfield unpacks different dimensions of this metacognitive skill. In fact, it’s probably more accurate to say that critical thinking is an umbrella term for an array of metacognitive skills.

Critical thinking allows us to be more discerning in how we channelize our time and attention while enabling us to make cogent arguments. It equips us to identify lacunae in thinking, whether in ourselves or in others, and alerts us to pause and ask the right questions when required. Though none of us can be entirely objective, it enables us to put aside our biases and assumptions when we’re presented with facts that dispute our worldviews. Critical thinking also helps us evaluate information from multiple perspectives, keeping contextual factors in mind.

Slowing down

In his book Thinking, Fast and Slow, Daniel Kahneman posits that human decision-making is subserved by two systems. System 1, which is fast and automatic, is often prone to cognitive biases. System 2, on the other hand, which is slow and deliberate, is better at logical reasoning. While System 1 is efficient, it is also prone to errors. So Chatfield says that if we want to hone our critical thinking skills, we must “slow down.”

We also need to pay attention deliberately which involves “noticing, engaging and grasping” ideas before evaluating them. This also entails filtering out the many distractions that are constantly vying for our attention in our digitally interconnected lives. As there is a copious amount of information vying for our attention, we need to exercise discretion when deciding how and to what we direct our attention.

What critical thinking entails

Arguments are one of the central features of critical thinking. Typically, an argument consists of a “line of reasoning” that tries to persuade you to accept a particular ‘conclusion.’ Whereas an assertion is just a claim, an argument involves an assertion and a conclusion. Critical thinking helps us assess whether an argument is sound.

A critical thinker will also be able to identify and sort different types of information. In addition to arguments, we may encounter descriptions, summaries, opinions, beliefs, clarifications, illustrations, and explanations. Critical thinking allows us to both evaluate and provide accurate descriptions that spell out relevant facts as “neutrally as possible.” Likewise, a clear and concise summary lists key points while omitting extraneous information.

Opinions and beliefs involve “preferences and judgments” that extend beyond objective facts. Critical thinking impels us to understand why a person may hold a particular belief or opinion. Further, we may see how a certain topic or issue maybe perceived differently by people with varying beliefs and opinions. As we acknowledge and appreciate other points of view, we may ask for and provide clarification to ensure that we mean the same thing when referring to particular terms. Critical thinking also helps us assess which explanation, among various options, fits best with the current evidence.

Such thinking also alerts us to biases in reasoning, including our own. One of the cognitive biases that we are prone to is confirmation bias, which often clouds critical thinking. It refers to a human inclination to seek out “new information” that corroborates our views while ignoring evidence to the contrary. While we all fall prey to this bias, critical thinking can help us be more wary and reduce the likelihood of us succumbing to it.

What teachers can do

Given that critical reasoning impacts our ability to perform and problem-solve, what can teachers do to equip students to acquire these metacognitive skills? Cognitive psychologist, Daniel Willingham, avers that critical thinking skills can be taught; however, he believes that they are not a generic set of skills that can be applied across domains. In the Occasional Paper Series published by the Department of Education, New South Wales, Willingham writes that the terms “analysis, synthesis and evaluation mean different things in different disciplines.” Thus, critical thinking cannot be divorced from a discipline and requires domain-specific knowledge.

Willingham says that teachers need to identify the relevant critical thinking skills required by their domain or subject. If you are a history teacher, you would want students to understand that history is always told from someone’s perspective. Whose viewpoint is being put forth? What sources is the historian using? Are any sources being omitted? Will the narrative be different if it is told from someone else’s point of view? In contrast, a critical reading of a scientific paper involves identifying the hypothesis, evaluating the evidence collected, and analyzing whether the conclusion follows from it.

In an article in College Teaching, Australian researcher, Tim Van Gelder, agrees with Willingham that critical thinking skills, though hard to acquire, can be taught. Like any skill, it requires practice where students get to exercise their critical thinking muscles on an ongoing basis and receive feedback to help them hone their thinking. Further, teachers need to “teach for transfer” because students don’t necessarily apply them across contexts, let alone domains. Teachers may also help students make connections between theoretical knowledge and its practical applications. If a lesson on sound in physics can be linked with musical instruments that students play, then they will form more robust linkages between academic knowledge to their playing of the guitar or keyboard.

To help students communicate their ideas effectively, Van Gelder suggests mapping their reasoning diagrammatically so that they can visually see if one idea connects to the next. Finally, we can ask students to check for biases in their own thinking by pointing out the most common types of thinking errors, including confirmation bias.

If the goal of education is to help students become self-directed learners, we need to emphasize critical thinking in all subjects be it art, botany, computer science, economics, or zoology.

References

  • Chatfield, T. (2022) Critical thinking. London: Sage Publications Ltd.
  • Kahneman, D. (2011) Thinking, fast and slow. New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux.
  • Van Gelder, T. Teaching critical thinking: Some lessons from cognitive science, College Teaching; Winter 2005.
  • Willingham D. (2019) How to teach critical thinking, Occasional paper series, State of New South Wales, Department of Education.

Aruna Sankaranarayanan is the author of Zero Limits and the co-author of Bee-Witched along with Brinda S. Narayan. She blogs at www.arunasankaranarayanan.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aruna Sankaranarayanan
What does it really mean to think critically? Aruna Sankaranarayanan writes that it begins with students learning to spot their own flawed arguments and biases. Here’s why it is a valuable skill in every subject.

item.title: My journey as a Hindi teacher
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Perminder Kaur

When I first enrolled in this reflective learner course (Mistakes or Missed takes) five months ago, I felt a wave of apprehension wash over me. The prospect of additional workload was daunting, and I questioned how I would manage my time and implement new techniques while ensuring my students learned effectively. However, as the weeks progressed, my concerns transformed into valuable insights and practical strategies.

As a Hindi teacher working in South India, I observed various recurring challenges among my students: frequent spelling errors, difficulty comprehending questions, incorrect pronunciation, issues with punctuation marks (viram chihn), and mistakes related to gender and number. These were not obstacles; they turned into opportunities for growth – both for my students and me.

Initial steps

I began implementing strategies from my course with my grade IX students. In the beginning, I presented them with a dummy sheet containing numerous errors and asked them how many mistakes they could identify and what score they would give if they were grading the worksheet. My students successfully identified the mistakes and suggested the marks that could be given. When I asked them why the person who filled this sheet might have made those errors, some provided excellent reasons. Hearing this made me happy, as it meant that the students were able to recognize mistakes and their underlying causes.

Figure 1: Dummy sheet

I wrote down their reasons on the board and then showed them a corrected version of the sheet on the screen, asking how many marks this deserved. The students compared both sheets and understood the differences – a starting point for recognizing mistakes and their underlying reasons.

Figure 2: Commonly made mistakes

I also shared stories from my childhood about how I was often confused by certain words, sometimes not comprehending the difference even after being corrected.

Weeks two to four

For two weeks, we dedicated 10 minutes daily to analyzing practice sheets together, identifying mistakes and discussing the reasons. Gradually, I noticed sharing of views and open discussion of mistakes even by those students who did not participate actively in class. They could be seen referring to their notebooks and worksheets to talk about their errors and the reasons behind them. I have started to feel a change in my classroom over the three weeks. It was a positive beginning; now all the students share their views and openly discuss their mistakes. In the fourth week, I introduced peer correction, which everyone embraced wholeheartedly. I was heartened to note that when a student highlighted another student's mistakes, it obviously meant that they were able to recognize those mistakes.

Figure 3: Peer correction

Remedial work was carried out to address the mistakes. The key approach was to have students write the incorrect words and then the correct ones next to them. This had the benefit of helping them clearly understand their mistakes, and as a result, their writing started to show fewer errors over time. Although the process was demanding, positive results began to show in just a few days.

Figure 4: Remedial work

Week five

In the fifth week, the students analyzed their notebooks and created a list of recurring mistakes. In a one-on-one session, my facilitator suggested that the students put up this list in a place where they would frequently see it. Following her advice, I encouraged the students to place their individual lists near their study tables, by their mirrors, or in places that they often look at, so that they could easily refer to it. As a result, the common mistakes in their upcoming writing topics began to decrease. Initially, the mistakes ranged from 20 to 25, but by the end of five weeks, they were reduced to 8 to 12.

Figure 5: Students’ mistakes list

There were 3-4 students in my class who rarely spoke, but even they started making lists and gradually began working on them. I asked the students to

  1. send five words of their choice twice a week to Google Classroom,
  2. form sentences using those words the following week,
  3. then write a short paragraph of 5-6 lines on them.

Within 2-3 weeks, I started to see the results; the students began to show confidence. In class tests, while they still made mistakes, they began attempting to write answers, something they previously wouldn’t even try.

Four students in my class, who initially struggled with Hindi reading, gradually began to develop an interest. I introduced them to a word game in Google Classroom and gave them jumbled sentences, which they corrected, even as they had fun. [Figure 6 shows one such example of a jumbled sentence which they had to rearrange correctly, and another exercise where deliberate errors in gender and number were made. Students had to identify and correct them.] This approach made the learning process enjoyable. Later, I gave them a short passage with mistakes, and they were able to correct it with 85% accuracy. This was truly an achievement for both them and me!

Figure 6: The worksheet with deliberate errors that was used for identifying mistakes

This was a great change that encouraged me as well. I sent the children some fun jokes, cartoons, and short stories for reading. They began to read these aloud and record their voices to send back to me. This way, their interest in reading improved alongside entertainment.

Figure 7: Reading activity in Google Classroom

Games and fun while learning

Most of the students in the south come from non-Hindi speaking backgrounds. To spark their interest in Hindi, I had a one-on-one session with my facilitator, who suggested several fun activities. I applied these activities in class, and my students really enjoyed them. Now, they eagerly look forward to Hindi class. On days when the Hindi class is missed due to another session or activity, they feel sad.

The tongue twister activity, in particular, was a big hit; everyone participated with enthusiasm, competing and having a lot of fun. Additionally, activities like role-playing, interviewing each other in Hindi, and technology-related tasks also engaged the students. One task, where they had to make-up and say dialogues for a silent video clip, was especially enjoyable.

Figure 8: Tongue twister activity

Gradually, the class environment became much more positive. Students began waiting eagerly for their homework assignments in Google Classroom (GCR), as their attitude towards Hindi slowly changed. I began sharing some songs in GCR, and after listening to them, they would answer two or three questions, sometimes in writing and other times orally, which was real fun.

Figure 9: Listening activity

After completing each lesson, I began asking the students to give a summary of that lesson in a radio jockey style, which turned out to be very entertaining. Even those who were usually quiet participated in this activity, speaking in broken Hindi. To keep everyone focused during reading, I introduced a "Kho-Kho Reading" activity. One student would start reading from the book and suddenly I would call out another student’s name, who would then continue reading from the exact point where the previous student left off. This would go on, with random names being called out, and it kept everyone alert and engaged, making them eager to read so they wouldn't miss their turn.

Through these small activities, the class became a joyful space where everyone shared their thoughts and ideas without hesitation and had engaging discussions.

Significant shifts

Once the students developed an interest, their writing skills started improving, and the number of mistakes reduced. For our mid-term assessment, we set a goal that no student would make mistakes with the usage of "में" (the preprosition ‘in’) and "मैं" (the personal pronoun ‘I’), and we succeeded in this mission with 90% success. To achieve this, we conducted small practice activities through practice sheets, which helped reinforce the correct usage and contributed to their progress.

Figure 10: Practice sheet

At the beginning of the session, I created a baseline and divided the students into three groups: high-performance students in green, average students in orange, and low-performance students in red, based on their initial performance data. This is shown in Figure 11. (Needless to say, this was purely for my own records, students were unaware of this.) Over time, I have seen gradual improvements, and I am hopeful that within the next two months, the red group will be reduced to zero. (The last column will be filled after the annual exam.) As a teacher and mentor, achieving this progress will bring me the greatest sense of satisfaction and happiness.

Figure 11: Progress tracking and performance grouping for student improvement

(WT - weekly test, MYA - midyear assessment)

The course, Seeing Missed Takes in Mistakes, has provided me with invaluable guidance as a language teacher. I am now encouraging my students to engage in proofreading, and with a little help, they are independently practicing peer and self-correction. They are able to recognize their mistakes, and most importantly, openly discuss them, which is a great achievement. When we realize our mistakes, we work with dedication to correct them.

Earlier, while teaching personalized learning to my class, I struggled to understand how to begin and how to assist each student effectively. During this time, the reflective learner course proved to be the perfect solution, like the "cherry on top." Though the course has ended, my journey continues.

Perminder Kaur is Head of Department, Hindi at Suchitra Academy, Hyderabad. She can be reached at perminder.k@suchitra.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Perminder Kaur
What if every mistake was a learning opportunity? Perminder Kaur shares how she used games and activities to make errors joyful in her classroom.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content:
Thanks Parminder mam for good article ,which is simple and easy to implement and high on output.
wing commander ramesh

item.title: Nothing ‘extra’ about these activities
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ajaa

After over two decades, I recently attended the local fair in my hometown. It’s called the Basant Mela or Spring Fest. The young girls getting ready for a dance performance held my attention. I used to be this girl, often on stage, ready to perform. I was also in the orchestra for my school house and played the guitar. I often took on the role of the narrator during class dramas. I used to paint and participate in sports activities as well. Come to think of it, I used to be present for every extra-curricular activity that I could possibly be a part of and which would release me from the routine of the classroom. My career aspirations included news reading, making railway announcements, performing in a theatre, and investigative journalism. But during my time in school, any career unrelated to science or engineering was not considered worth talking about. I was average in academics, failed in maths, flourished in the languages, and managed my way through school because of rote memorization. But thanks to the very predictable patterns of exams, I also managed maths pretty well in the boards.

For most of my career as a development professional in education, I have interacted with government schools across the country. Only a few have extra-curricular activities on a regular basis, therefore I always make it a point to bring something extra-curricular to the schools I go to. Children keenly line-up and give their names to participate in these activities. I see a little bit of me in them.

Teachers like outsiders like me organize these activities. While they really appreciate what I do, there is little interest in taking ownership of any of these extra-curricular activities. This is not just about the teachers, but about the way our system runs and what it prioritizes.

As a professional in the education space, I have observed how learning outcomes and subject-related support always overpower the extra-curricular. There is a certain divide between those who work for learning outcomes and those who work beyond that academic space. The former feel they are doing something important. Even in the private school that I studied in, the extra-curricular teachers (mostly a subject teacher too) would make a comment about the academics needing more attention. So, even schools that do have these spaces do not prioritize it. There might be exceptions, but that’s surely only a handful.

Despite my interest in the extra-curricular, I quit most of the activities with the onset of menstruation, and the rest as the pressure of academics started mounting in high school.

But from my experience of actively participating in extra-curricular activities and organizing such spaces for children, I have learnt that there are quite a few key learnings that we can take away from the extra-curricular field, which will benefit everyone.

A place to be and bond

These are spaces for teachers too to open-up, bond, and share a few laughs with the children. This has a great impact on the relationship that is shared inside the classroom. I have observed that teachers who engage with extra-curricular activities are more invested in the journey of their students. Their relationship is no longer mechanical and limited to the subject. Children also tend to open-up to those teachers with whom they are able to break the boundaries. These are often the teachers who stand out in our memories too.

Practicing life skills

Life skills, 21st century skills, whatever we want to call them, extra-curricular activities give space for team work, collaboration, resolving conflicts, and creative thinking. While these skills may not be mentioned on any certificate, these are skills necessary to live a productive life.

Seeing beyond academics

For those who do not fit the set standards in academics, extra-curricular activities offer an alternative space to find their identity.

Opening up to different ways of being

Whether in a classroom, at home, or in public, we are expected to behave in a certain way. Extra-curricular activities can break such expectations. These spaces are a great way to socialize and learn behavioural dynamics which are often controlled in other spaces.

The pull towards school

Anything beyond the regular classes where children find a space for themselves, pulls them to come and engage with schooling. There is a value to these experiences that we hold dear in our hearts and minds.

Therapeutic effects

Extra-curricular activities are a good way to engage and channelize energies into something meaningful and constructive. They are an opportunity for children to disengage with the monotonous, find their space, and connect with others. These activities offer a much needed pathway to form one’s identity beyond the one based on marks and merit.

Finally, school is not the only space for extra-curricular activities just because the word curricular is attached to it! I have seen children find their own ways of play, activity, and engagement at home, in colonies, in traditional societies, and all these are also equally important. There is nothing ‘extra’ about these very necessary activities. I hope that someday this name changes too.

Ajaa has been a development professional for the last 18 years. She has mostly worked with government schools and community programs across geographies through non-profit organizations, encouraging experiential spaces to practice 21st century skills in schools. A children’s literature enthusiast, she has capacitated several educators working in schools and communities to create spaces for children to express and experiment with thoughts. She can be reached at ajaa.sharma@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ajaa
School is about much more than lesson plans and marks. Ajaa Sharma writes about how extra-curricular activities offer children and teachers much-needed spaces to play and bond.

item.title: Bringing business and entrepreneurship into the classroom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Shalini Solanki

In today's fast-changing world, it's important for students to learn skills that go beyond mere classroom application. One such crucial area is business and entrepreneurship, which offers students insights into real-world scenarios, problem-solving, and a sense of initiative. As educators, integrating business concepts into the English classroom not only enhances language skills but also cultivates a mindset of innovation and creativity.

As a teacher of grade 6 students following the Cambridge curriculum, I encountered the challenge of teaching business vocabulary and concepts in an engaging manner. With limited expertise in this area, I embarked on a journey of exploration and discovery to create a meaningful learning experience for my students.

A key strategy I employed was to leverage active learning methods to bring the topic alive in the classroom. Rather than relying solely on textbooks, I sought to integrate real-world examples and experiences into the lessons.

One such breakthrough moment occurred when I stumbled upon the word 'start-up' while exploring business vocabulary with my students. Coincidentally, a colleague was discussing episodes of the television show Shark Tank, where entrepreneurs pitch their ideas to a panel of investors. Inspired by this, I devised an interactive activity to simulate the entrepreneurial experience in our classroom.

The activity involved students working in teams to develop their own 'start-up' business idea. They were tasked with conducting market research, identifying target audiences, creating a business plan, and presenting their ideas to the class. Much like the entrepreneurs on Shark Tank, students had to defend their business proposals and address questions and feedback from their peers.

This hands-on approach not only reinforced their business vocabulary, but also fostered essential skills such as critical thinking, communication, and teamwork. Moreover, it sparked their imagination and entrepreneurial spirit, igniting a passion for innovation and problem-solving.

During our Shark Tank inspired activity, one group presented a unique and eco-friendly business idea: a footwear cleaner made from recycled plastic bottles and boxes. Their pitch began with a problem we could all relate to: dirty shoes after playtime. To keep the classroom tidy, they proposed their innovative solution.

With a flair for the dramatic, they announced that their product had already sold nine units, boasting a profitable venture. They used words like "shares" and talked about support from (pretend) companies, which made their pitch seem more real.

Their enthusiasm was infectious as they described their product's environmental benefits and demonstrated its effectiveness. The judges, played by fellow classmates, posed challenging questions, mirroring the dynamic exchanges seen on Shark Tank. The group confidently defended their business plan, showcasing their understanding of market demand and sustainability.

Their presentation wasn't just creative, it also showed how businesses can help the environment. It was a great example of how students can come up with smart ideas that also do good for the world.

Another example: one group created an infant kit for new mothers. The kit included handmade toys and colourful picture books for babies' cognitive development. The students made these books using recycled paper, which they turned into paste, dried, and then painted. The kit also included a box of earrings for the mothers. They even had a discount code for the product, allowing mothers to apply for it and receive a further discount on the product.

When discussing the discount, the group presented detailed calculations to the "sharks", showing how they would still make a profit and what their estimated growth would be. This was possible, thanks to our maths teacher, who taught them about profit and loss.

Incorporating business and entrepreneurship into the English classroom not only enhanced language proficiency, but also cultivated a mindset of curiosity, resilience, and adaptability. By providing students with opportunities to explore real-world challenges and develop practical skills, we empower them to thrive in an ever-changing global landscape.

The outcome of this activity was highly positive. Students not only gained a deeper understanding of business concepts, but also developed practical skills such as public speaking, collaboration, and financial literacy. They were enthusiastic and engaged throughout the project, and their confidence grew as they presented their ideas and received feedback. Additionally, this activity fostered a sense of empathy and social responsibility, as students designed products to help young mothers.

Integrating business and entrepreneurship into the English classroom offered a rich and rewarding learning experience for the students. Through active learning methods, real-world examples, and hands-on activities, we can inspire the next generation of innovators, problem-solvers, and leaders.

As educators, let us seize the opportunity to ignite a passion for business and entrepreneurship in our students, equipping them with the skills and mindset to succeed in the 21st century and beyond.

Shalini Solanki is currently pursuing her master’s in Education Management at King’s College London. Previously, she worked as a team lead and programme facilitator for English at an international school in Vijayawada, Andhra Pradesh. In her free time, she enjoys reading children’s literature, doing yoga, and watching historical films. She can be reached at shalinisolanki70@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shalini Solanki
A Grade 6 English classroom became a launchpad for innovation. Shalini Solanki shares how her students pitched their ideas, while learning about business through English.
